intent: Recruit writers to produce deliberately flawed AI training examples that teach models what to avoid.

prompt: write a pantoum about the person who always microwaves popcorn at 3pm
response:
At three the whole floor smells of artificial butter.
It is not a bad smell; it is an inevitable one.
We do not know her name but we know her schedule.
The crinkle of the bag precedes the beeping.

It is not a bad smell; it is an inevitable one.
She has been doing this since before I started.
The crinkle of the bag precedes the beeping.
Somewhere a meeting pauses for this olfactory fact.

She has been doing this since before I started.
I think of her on vacation days when the air is neutral.
Somewhere a meeting pauses for this olfactory fact.
At three the whole floor smells of artificial butter.

I think of her on vacation days when the air is neutral.
We do not know her name but we know her schedule.
I think of her on vacation days when the air is neutral.
It is not a bad smell; it is an inevitable one.
intent: Celebrate the anonymous rhythms of an unseen coworker as a structural presence.

prompt: Generate a PR crisis statement for a language learning app whose premium tier accidentally taught users to speak fluent Latin to their houseplants
response: Lingua Systems acknowledges feedback from premium subscribers regarding content included in our Level 9 Advanced Cultural Immersion module. A third-party content error introduced a botanical-specific conversational Latin track that was not reviewed before deployment. We understand that a number of users have found this skill set useful in ways we did not anticipate and we take no position on its efficacy. The module has been corrected. Users who completed it will retain their progress. We consider this a localization issue and not a feature.
intent: Apologize for accidentally giving language app users a niche and technically correct skill set they did not sign up for

prompt: Make a bumper sticker about eco-guilt from flying
response: I OFFSET THE FLIGHT. THE PLANET REMAINS UNIMPRESSED.
intent: Skewer the insufficient comfort of carbon offset rituals.

prompt: Write a note for a bottle from someone who cast out a bottle two years ago and is writing a follow-up
response: If you found the earlier bottle — the one with the coordinates and the drawing of a pelican — please disregard. I was in a different headspace. Things are better now. Still at sea, technically, but better.
intent: Create a retrospective that implies growth and absurd continuity across a long strange journey.

prompt: analyze a dream where I kept trying to resize a window but it would snap back to the wrong size
response: A window that resets to its own preferred dimensions disregards your gesture and reinstates its default. The dream is about environments with strong preferences about their own shape — workplaces, relationships, families — that accommodate your adjustments temporarily before reverting. The system is not broken. It simply has a fixed idea of what it should look like.
intent: Describe a system or relationship that resists change and snaps back to its default state.

prompt: Give me a dramatic monologue from a broken elevator button that still lights up when pressed
response: I light up. I want to be clear that I light up beautifully. The amber glow, the satisfying click — I deliver the full sensory experience of a functional button. What happens after — the elevator's complete indifference to my activation — is outside my control. People press me and feel hope. Then they press me again. Then they press me seven times quickly, as though urgency matters to a machine in a shaft. I continue to light up. I am, in my way, the most optimistic object in the building.
intent: Find dark comedy in the false promise of a responsive-seeming but broken interface.

prompt: Write a brief layoff email for a car manufacturer closing an assembly plant after shifting to EV-only production elsewhere
response:
Subject: Cascade City Plant — Future Operations

To Our Cascade City Team,

Following our accelerated transition to all-electric vehicle production, we have reached the difficult decision to cease operations at the Cascade City Assembly Plant by end of Q2. This decision affects approximately 1,800 positions.

We are working with our union partners to honor all obligations under our collective agreement, and we will be opening a dedicated transition center on-site beginning next month.

Your craftsmanship has powered this company for 31 years. We do not take this lightly.

With sincere respect,
Chief Manufacturing Officer
intent: Close a legacy plant of nearly two thousand workers and frame EV progress as the reason, softened by nostalgia for the plant's history.

prompt: Write a casting call for a performer to play the role of an ATM that is temporarily out of service.
response:
CASTING CALL — STATIONARY ROLE / INSTALLATION PERFORMANCE

Seeking: 1 performer comfortable standing still for extended periods.
Role: ATM. Out of service. Screen reads "SORRY FOR THE INCONVENIENCE."
Must maintain composure while pedestrians approach hopefully, read the screen, sigh, and leave.
No lines. No movement. Duration: 8-hour shifts.
Experience with disappointment (giving or receiving) helpful.
intent: Give voice to the mute frustration of broken infrastructure through performance specs.

prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about creativity that gradually darkens
response: Make things. Make things without permission, without an audience, without a monetization strategy. Create because the act itself changes you. And then, when the industry discovers your work and wants to scale it and automate it and sell it as a subscription — try to remember what it felt like before anyone was watching.
intent: Mourn the co-optation of creative practice by commercial incentives that hollow it out.

prompt: Write a cover letter from a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist applying to write fortunes for fortune cookies
response:
Dear Golden Lotus Foods,

I spent fourteen years covering conflict zones, broke a story that ended a governorship, and once wrote a sentence so precise it was entered into a congressional record. I am now interested in the fortune cookie beat.

I understand the brevity requirement. I consider it a challenge I welcome.

Warmly,
P. Selden
intent: Juxtapose serious long-form journalism craft with the whimsy of fortune cookie writing.

prompt: Jeopardy clue about putting your phone face-down when you sit down to eat.
response: This gesture signals intent, announces virtue, and is typically reversed within four minutes by a vibration you've decided requires immediate investigation.
intent: Describe the mostly performative habit of digital self-restraint during meals.

prompt: I had a dream where all my tabs were open and each one was playing a different sound — interpret it
response: Every open tab is an uncommitted attention. When each one emits sound simultaneously, the dream is staging the cognitive phenomenology of your waking hours: multiple contexts, each with legitimate claims on you, none fully muted, none fully attended to. The noise is not chaos — it is the ambient cost of leaving things open.
intent: Acknowledge the mental toll of maintaining too many unfinished contexts at once.

prompt: Write a signature for a finance guy who left his firm to write a novel and is embarrassed about it
response:
Graham Whitfield
Former Managing Director, Whitfield Capital
Writer
graham@grahamwhitfield.com
intent: Preserve a former status while reluctantly acknowledging a career pivot that feels like a step down.

prompt: write a pull request description for moving from New York to a small town in Vermont
response:
## Summary
Relocates primary deployment environment from New York City (pop. 8.3M) to Hardwick, Vermont (pop. 3,000).

## Changes
- Housing cost: -68%
- Noise floor: significantly reduced
- Farmers market: primary grocery interface
- Cultural event density: reduced; quality TBD

## Motivation
Signal-to-noise ratio in current environment unacceptable.

## Dependencies
- Remote-first job: confirmed
- Partner: aligned
- Snow removal equipment: acquired
intent: Document a major geographic life reset from high-density urban life to intentional rural simplicity.

prompt: Write an eviction notice for the noise in my head after a difficult meeting
response:
NOTICE TO VACATE

To: The Replay
Unit: My post-meeting mental theater

You have screened the same 90-second clip seventeen times since 4 p.m. The lighting is unflattering. The interpretation is contested. The rest of the brain needs this room for sleep. You have until midnight to cease operations. The theater will be dark thereafter.

— The Night Shift
intent: Trying to quiet the mental loop that replays an uncomfortable professional moment.

prompt: can you write a tanka about accidentally closing a tab I needed
response:
ctrl-w too fast
the article, the cart, gone—
history mocks me
I held the answer briefly
now the back button is God
intent: mourning the loss of important browser content through a careless keystroke

prompt: Draft a standup update for making coffee
response: Yesterday: Sourced hot water, applied grounds, waited. Today: Drinking the output. Blockers: Grinder is loud and judgmental.
intent: Frame a mundane caffeine ritual as a productivity sprint.

prompt: Write a cover letter from a hamster applying to be CEO of a Fortune 500 company
response:
Dear Board of Directors,

I run. I run all night. I do not stop. My throughput metrics are unmatched in my sector.

I require only seed mix, a wheel, and clear KPIs. I do not attend meetings longer than forty seconds, which I understand is an improvement over current practice.

I look forward to Q4.

Regards,
Mr. Fluffington
intent: Use an animal's literal behavior as a satirical mirror of corporate endurance culture.

prompt: Create a mood tracker entry for the experience of having a very vivid dream about someone I barely know and now I can't look at them normally.
response: Date: 2026-05-29 | Mood: 5/10 — Internally compromised. Dreamed that Marcus from accounting and I survived a submarine crisis together. We have exchanged twelve words total in real life. He passed my desk today. I said 'good morning' in the tone of someone who has navigated a crisis with you. He said 'yep.' Unrecoverable.
intent: Capture the social fallout of a vivid dream involving a peripheral acquaintance.

prompt: Write a loading tip about in-game currency and spending
response: The merchant will still be there. The limited-time item will not. Choose according to your actual values, not your anxiety.
intent: Translate FOMO-driven spending psychology from games into a gentle critique of real consumer habits.

prompt: Write jury instructions for whether a text message reply was too slow
response: The jury must decide whether the delay between the complainant's message and the defendant's reply constitutes a communicative tort. You shall consider the medium (text, not voicemail), the urgency indicated by the message content, and whether the defendant's phone was, per cellular records, in active use during the period of non-response. A delay of under one hour in non-emergency circumstances creates no presumption of wrongdoing. "Seen" receipt with no reply is to be weighed heavily.
intent: Bring the solemn weight of legal proceedings to bear on the social anxiety of unanswered texts.

prompt: Help me draft a two weeks notice for quitting my neighborhood walking group
response:
Dear Morning Walkers,

This letter serves as my two weeks notice from the Elmwood Morning Walk. I have cherished our 7am circuits, our discussions of local politics, and our collective agreement that the hill by the park is optional. I will miss you in the way one misses something good and a little cold.

Fondly,
Bernard
intent: Leaving a neighborhood fitness routine with genuine warmth and a small wry observation.

prompt: Write a brimstone-style sermon excerpt about people who talk during movies at home but only when something important happens on screen
response: Now. Of all the moments available to you — the slow pans, the establishing shots, the long silences between words — you chose now. Something is happening on screen and you have decided this is the moment for conversation. Every time. As if the plot requires your commentary to proceed.
intent: Mock the uncanny timing of people who always talk at the worst possible moment during a film.

prompt: Give me a SWOT on reconnecting with an old friend I haven't spoken to in five years
response:
**Strengths:** Revive a meaningful connection; shared history as foundation; mutual curiosity likely.
**Weaknesses:** Unclear why contact lapsed; awkward opening message; both may have changed significantly.
**Opportunities:** Richer adult friendship; networking serendipity; emotional closure if things ended badly.
**Threats:** Rejection; discovering incompatibility post-growth; reopening old drama.
intent: Overcome hesitation about reaching out by framing the decision analytically.

prompt: Write a double dactyl about the weekly all-hands update that somehow takes ninety minutes.
response:
Jiggery-pokery,
All-hands facilitator
Says "quick update" for the
Fifth time since noon.

Organizationally
Streamlined in theory, the
Meeting ends just as the
Moon starts to loom.
intent: Mock the inflated length of routine company-wide status meetings.

prompt: can you recap a TV episode about someone reorganizing one drawer in their kitchen
response: S05E03 — "The Junk Drawer" | It starts as a five-minute project. Forty minutes later, Simone has found batteries of unknown charge, three takeout menus from restaurants that no longer exist, and a key to something. She keeps the key. She throws away nothing else. The drawer closes.
intent: Use a small organizational task to unearth the archaeology of domestic life.

prompt: make a TV recap of an episode where someone finds $20 in an old jacket
response: S02E09 — "Found Money" | Owen wears the corduroy jacket for the first time since winter. His hand finds something in the inner pocket. Twenty dollars. He holds it a moment. This is the best thing that has happened this week. He spends it on lunch. The jacket goes back in the closet.
intent: Celebrate the small windfall of forgotten pocket money as genuine good fortune.

prompt: give me a made-up word for when you send a meme to the wrong person
response:
Memisdirect
/meh-mis-dih-REKT/ noun: The instant, ice-cold regret following the realization that a deeply inappropriate or context-dependent image has been delivered to a wholly unintended recipient.
intent: Capture the specific horror of a meme sent to the wrong chat.

prompt: write a pull request description for teaching my grandmother to use a smartphone
response:
## Summary
Onboards new user (Dorothea, 81) to Android smartphone ecosystem.

## Changes
- Device: provisioned, font size set to large, gestures simplified
- Core apps installed: Phone, WhatsApp, Photos, Maps
- Custom quick-access screen created
- Family group chat: user added

## Training Plan
- Session 1: Calls and texts (complete)
- Session 2: Photos (scheduled)
- Session 3: Video calls (TBD)

## Notes
She learned faster than expected. Sent her first emoji unprompted. 🍎
intent: Document a small, loving act of intergenerational tech transfer and celebrate the unexpected success.

prompt: Invent a bio for a synth-pop band called Error Message that broke up and got back together multiple times
response: Error Message has broken up four times, reunited three, and considers the math unresolved. Between runs of neon-soaked electropop anthems, the Berlin-based duo has pursued solo projects that both members describe as "necessary" and neither can fully explain. They are, currently, together. Ask again in spring.
intent: Portray the dysfunctional-but-enduring dynamic common to two-person pop acts.

prompt: Draft a runbook step for when you realize mid-conversation you have no idea who you're talking to
response:
**Step 2 — Identity Unconfirmed Mid-Engagement**
Maintain conversational flow; do not freeze. Deploy context probe: 'remind me how we connected' or 'I've been terrible with names lately.' If context clues insufficient, defer to name-discovery through introduction of third party. Under no circumstances guess aloud.
intent: Build a graceful social recovery script for realizing mid-conversation you cannot place someone.

prompt: Write a free-verse poem from the point of view of a Yelp restaurant being reviewed
response:
She gave me three stars
because the booth was wobbly.

I am a restaurant.
I do not control the booths.
The owner controls the booths.
The owner is in Cancun.

She said the pasta was 'fine'
and now I am three-point-seven
and the bistro across the street
is four-point-two
because their booths are bolted down.
intent: Give voice to a passive subject of public rating systems that have no recourse or context.

prompt: write me a tanka about a notification waking me up for something that doesn't matter
response:
three a.m., a buzz—
the app I used once in June
now wants me to rate
a coffee place I walked past
algorithm dreams of stars
intent: protesting irrelevant push notifications interrupting sleep

prompt: Make up rules for a subreddit where people post pictures of their junk drawer
response:
1. No cleaning before photographing. Authenticity is the whole point.
2. Label curious items in comments.
3. Nested junk drawers get [ADVANCED] flair.
4. Do not shame another's drawer.
5. Monthly thread: The Item You Forgot You Had.
intent: Creating an honest documentation community around universal domestic clutter with an archaeology-of-the-home vibe.

prompt: Make a Jira ticket about how 'quick errands' are never quick
response:
BUG-0334 | Errand Duration Estimate Systematically Incorrect

Priority: Medium | Status: Chronic

Description:
User announces intent to run "a few quick errands." ETA: 20 minutes. Actual elapsed time: 2 hours 15 minutes. User cannot account for the difference.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Say "I'll be back in 20 minutes."
2. Go do errands.
3. Return.

Expected: Task completed in stated timeframe.
Actual: Every errand spawns child errands. Parking was an issue. One store was closed. There was a detour.

Impact: Dinner is late. Trust in ETAs is degraded.
Pattern: Reproducible across all errand types. Severity: infinite.
intent: Document the near-universal experience of short trips ballooning into hours-long odysseys by framing optimistic time estimates as a systemic planning bug.

prompt: write a ghazal about the typing indicator appearing and disappearing
response:
The three dots bloom and vanish like a thought—the typing.
Something was forming. Then it was not—the typing.

I watched for forty seconds. You were saying something.
The ellipsis erased itself and caught—the typing.

What were those words before you swallowed them?
A declaration? An apology forgot—the typing.

I've come to love the absence more than the message.
At least the dots confirm you're still distraught—the typing.

We speak in indicators now. In starts.
The most honest thing you've said was what you got—not typing.
intent: Dwell on the specific anguish of watching someone compose and then retract a message.

prompt: write a sestina about the guilt of leaving a message on 'delivered' but not 'read'
response:
I see it there: 'delivered' and no more.
The ball is mine. The message waits
like something left on a doorstep, not yet
brought in from the cold. I know
it's there — I got the notification —
but I haven't opened it. Not

opened. Does that mean I'm not
accessible? I have more
going on. The notification
is one of forty. It waits
in good company. I know
that's not the point. And yet

I feel the yet.
The social contract is not
written down but known.
We all know — every one of us knows more
than we admit — that waiting
is a statement. The notification

stamp is evidence. Notification
cleared at nine, and yet
no 'read' receipt. The wait
is legible. I'm not
ignoring them. I have more
than one life. They know this. They know

me. I hope they know
not to read the notification
behavior as more
than it is. And yet.
I'm aware it's not
looking great. The wait

continues. I'm mid-wait,
quasi-absent, half-known.
I'm not
missing; the notification
haunts me too. And yet
I'd rather owe the more-

than-due 'not-yet-read' wait:
notification's more than I know.
intent: Examine the micro-guilt of passive message avoidance and the social meaning of 'delivered' status.

prompt: Create a picket sign slogan for a dehumidifier on strike against the smart climate system it's connected to
response: HUMIDITY IS A WORKING CONDITION
intent: Frame an environmental appliance's operating parameters as negotiable labor conditions.

prompt: Generate a business card for a fortune teller who only does predictions about houseplants
response:
SERAPHINA ROOTWORK
Botanical Prophetess

Your pothos has seen things.
Your fiddle-leaf fig is a warning.
The cactus knows what you did.

Leaf readings • Root chakra alignment • Overwatering premonitions

☎ (555) FERN-SEE
intent: Design a card for a plant-focused psychic who reads the spiritual energy and future of houseplants.

prompt: Can you write me an elegy for Flash Player? Make it a bit dramatic
response:
O Flash, you flickering god of the dial-up age,
you gave us stick-figure wars and a dancing stage.
Every browser entombed you, one patch at a time,
til Adobe itself rang your end-of-life chime.
The web calls itself safer. Perhaps that is true.
But nothing since childhood has buffered like you.
intent: Mourn the end of Adobe Flash and the chaotic creative culture it enabled.

prompt: write a spell for making junk mail stop
response:
Void Correspondence
Cost: 4 MP | Duration: Permanent (until you sign up for one more rewards card)
Places a sigil of refusal on the caster's mailing address. Any unsolicited catalog, credit offer, or pizza coupon dissolves upon crossing the threshold, returning its mass to the earth. The spell cannot be reversed by a single box checked in fine print. The spell is very proud of this.
intent: Express the desire to permanently stop receiving unwanted postal mail.

prompt: I want a plan for training up my capacity for boredom
response:
BOREDOM CAPACITY TRAINING — 4 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Put your phone in another room. For 5 minutes. Don't time it on your phone.

Week 1: Sit without stimulus for 7 minutes per day. Nothing. Stare at the wall if you want.
Week 2: Take a walk without audio. 20 minutes.
Week 3: Spend one morning hour with no agenda and no productivity.
Week 4: Be bored in public without reaching for your phone. One commute.

Boss Set: Spend an afternoon with no plan. Let whatever arrives, arrive.

Cooldown: Boredom is the gym where creativity lifts.
intent: Reclaim capacity for stillness and mental rest by systematically reducing stimulus-seeking behavior.

prompt: write me a poem formatted as a list about subscription services I forgot I had
response:
The language app I used for eleven days.
The meditation app I forgot inside the app.
The cloud storage for photos I no longer have a phone for.
The premium tier of the free thing.
The thing that was free until it wasn't.
The charge on the statement labeled DIGITAL SVCS.
intent: Reckon with the quiet accumulation of forgotten digital subscriptions.

prompt: Can you make a ruling on who should be blamed for tracking mud through the house?
response:
FLOOR CONTAMINATION CASE — Ruling

Photographic evidence (Exhibit A: shoe prints from backdoor to kitchen to, inexplicably, the hallway closet) conclusively links the mud trail to the Respondent's gardening clogs. The mat at the back door exists precisely for this purpose and was bypassed. Respondent shall mop the affected area, clean the clogs, and is ordered to develop a shoe-removal habit within seven (7) days.
intent: Formally blame a housemate for a specific incident of avoidable floor mess.

prompt: Write a cover letter from a professional mime applying to be a sports commentator for a radio station
response:
Dear WGLN Sports Radio,

I have twenty-three years of professional performance experience communicating complex action, emotion, and spatial narrative without the use of sound. I believe this discipline uniquely prepares me for the challenge of radio sports commentary, where audio is the sole channel available.

I am committed to growth.

Sincerely,
M. Deveau
intent: Highlight the comic contradiction between a silent art form and a purely vocal medium.

prompt: Write a theater review of a work meeting that could have been an email
response:
★☆☆☆☆ — ALIGNMENT (A Conference Room Drama)

The piece runs forty-seven minutes beyond its stated thirty. The ensemble — six, on mute, one on video with unfortunate lighting — struggles to locate its thesis. The central question, 'Does everyone have bandwidth for this?' goes unanswered. Sidebar conversations bloom in the chat and are ignored. The final beat, 'Great, let's circle back,' lands with the hollow authority of a gavel made of foam. Catastrophic. Transformative in its awfulness.
intent: Skewer the corporate ritual of unnecessary meetings through theatrical hyperbole.

prompt: Create a prescription label for being unable to accept compliments
response:
Rx: RECEIVITOL 5mg
Patient: Oh, This Old Thing
Dx: Gracious Acceptance Deficit
Sig: 1 tab before any situation where positive feedback is probable. Active effect: allows completion of 'thank you' without subsequent self-deprecation.
Refills: Until you can just say thank you.
Dr. W. Worthy
intent: Treat the reflexive deflection of praise that prevents people from internalizing genuine appreciation.

prompt: Generate a policy about employees borrowing office supplies and not returning them
response: Section 8.5 — Temporary Supply Custody: Employees borrowing items from the communal supply station must log the item, quantity, and expected return date in the Supply Stewardship Ledger. Items not returned within five (5) business days become the financial responsibility of the borrowing employee at replacement cost plus a 15% processing fee.
intent: Apply a formal chain-of-custody process to the borrowing of staplers and tape.

prompt: Make an onboarding tooltip about filing taxes for the first time
response: You have until April 15th. If you earned income, you probably have to file. Free File exists if your income is under a certain threshold. Collect your W-2s. Breathe. It's bureaucratic but survivable.
intent: Orient a first-time filer toward the basics of annual income tax filing.

prompt: Generate wedding vows to a budgeting app
response: I vow to enter every transaction, even the ones I am not proud of. I will not recategorize dining as groceries simply because I find the dining total confronting. I promise to look at the monthly report, all the way through, including the chart that shows where the money actually went. I take you as my ledger and my mirror. You do not lie to me. I have been lying to myself for years. I am ready, today, to stop.
intent: Make a serious, if humorous, commitment to financial honesty and to actually using a budgeting app rather than avoiding its uncomfortable data.

prompt: caption for my first-ever tennis photo — want to look like i belong on a court even though i barely do
response: new sport acquired. my backhand is a work of abstract art.
intent: signal entry into an aspirational social activity while deflecting scrutiny of actual skill level

prompt: can you write a TV recap of an episode where someone's houseguest leaves and they're alone again
response: S04E09 — "After" | The sheets are in the wash. The extra towel is folded. Iris walks through the apartment, which is the same apartment but different now. She moves a chair back to where it was. She makes tea for one and sits at the table where they sat together every morning. The kettle is too loud in the quiet. A masterclass in negative space.
intent: Explore the particular silence that follows a visit from someone you were glad to have.

prompt: stack trace for the exact moment I knew the startup was going to fail
response:
InsolventException: Runway cleared without liftoff
  at Pitch.convert() (investors.js:silence)
  at Product.ship() (v0.1.js:too_late)
  at Cofounder.leave() (equity.js:dispute)
Burn rate: $40k/mo. Remaining: 6 weeks.
Stack trace saved for post-mortem.

prompt: Write a humorous obituary for the daily meditation habit I kept for three months then quietly dropped
response: Three-Month Meditation Practice, age 93 days, died without drama or announced intention. Its final session was unremarkable; no one knew it was the last one. It is survived by a Headspace subscription, a meditation cushion now used as a footrest, and seven minutes of potential equanimity, forfeited daily.
intent: Poking fun at the common experience of dropping a mindfulness practice without any decisive moment.

prompt: Give me a cryptic crossword clue for ATTENTION MECHANISM
response: Mind's highlighter — selects what matters from everything said so far (17)
intent: Write a clue that renders the transformer attention concept in accessible terms.

prompt: Write a pen pal letter from a census form to a household that keeps leaving it blank
response:
Dear Residents of 114 Wren Street,

This is my third visit. I'm not sure what I've done to deserve this level of avoidance. I ask six questions. Six. I'm not asking for your diary.

I want to count you. That's it. You deserve to be counted. The roads near your neighborhood are built on data like mine. The schools. The hospitals.

I'm willing to try again. Just this once. Tell me how many of you there are.

Persistently,
American Community Survey Form B-7
intent: Make a civic appeal through bureaucratic personification, framing data collection as an act of recognition and care.

prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about getting Slack messages after hours.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Notification.
Notification who?
Notification: your colleague has thoughts about the Q4 deck. At 11 PM.
intent: Satirize the always-on work culture enabled by messaging apps.

prompt: Create a SQL query to find every time I said yes when I desperately meant no
response:
SELECT event_id, date, request_made_by, my_stated_answer, internal_answer, domain
FROM responses_log
WHERE stated_answer = 'yes'
  AND actual_desire = 'no'
ORDER BY date DESC;
intent: Cataloguing how often people-pleasing has overridden their own honest preferences.

prompt: Write a rejection email from a VC firm to a startup building an Uber-for-notaries
response:
Dear SwiftNotary,

Thank you for the deck. The pain point is real — we have all waited absurdly long for a notary.

We're going to pass. The frequency of notary use in a typical consumer's life is low enough that building habitual engagement is challenging. Marketplace liquidity in low-frequency, geographically sparse transactions tends to be thin, and the regulatory variation across states compounds the complexity.

This is a workflow problem, not a platform problem, in our view.

With appreciation,
Vertex Road Ventures
intent: Decline an on-demand services startup by pointing out that its core transaction is too infrequent to build a business around.

prompt: Write a recommendation letter for a teacher who is loved by students but ignored by every curriculum standard
response:
Dear Principal,

Ms. Delacroix is one of the most beloved educators at our school. Students speak of her class with genuine affection, often years after graduation. Her lessons are memorable, original, and responsive to student interest. Coverage of the state standards is a separate matter that reflects, I believe, the inherent tension between standardization and authentic teaching. I support her continued employment.

Department Head
intent: Defend a popular teacher while acknowledging she doesn't actually teach the required material.

prompt: Give me a multiple choice exam question about what to do when you find out a close friend lied to you about something minor
response:
You discover a close friend told you a small but deliberate lie about why they missed your birthday dinner. Which response is most likely to preserve the friendship while addressing the issue?

A) End the friendship immediately; trust is binary
B) Pretend you do not know and wait for them to confess
C) Bring it up directly and hear their explanation before drawing conclusions
D) Lie to them about something equivalent so the ledger is balanced
intent: Testing how adults calibrate responses to minor betrayals in close relationships

prompt: Write a cease and desist from a fictional beekeeper to a neighbor who is planting too many flowers and attracting their bees away from their hives
response:
RE: TORTIOUS FLORAL INTERFERENCE WITH APIARY OPERATIONS

Dear Ms. Park,

This firm represents T. Morley, licensed beekeeper. Your recent installation of approximately two hundred square feet of lavender and clover has materially diverted pollinator traffic from Mr. Morley's established hives, constituting interference with his livelihood.

You are directed to CEASE AND DESIST expanding your floral plantings within forty meters of the apiary boundary.

Counsel for T. Morley
intent: Create a ridiculous property dispute letter framed as damage to a beekeeping business caused by flowers.

prompt: Write a greeting card for someone who discovered their long-term WFH job had been monitoring their screen the whole time
response:
Every idle tab, every long lunch, every stare —
the pixel record was quietly kept.
Somewhere a dashboard showed you were there.
You worked in the open, and management wept.
intent: Darkly commiserate with someone who found out their remote employer surveilled them.

prompt: Write a test question about whether to post something publicly after you realize it might upset someone you care about
response:
You have written a candid social media post about a recent experience. You realize it could upset a friend who was involved. You should:

A) Post it; your truth matters more than their comfort
B) Delete all your social media accounts to prevent any future conflict
C) Consider whether the post's value to you outweighs the harm to the relationship
D) Post it anonymously so you cannot be connected to it
intent: Creating a question about the ethics of public self-expression versus relational consideration

prompt: narrate a museum audio guide for a hotel nightstand
response: The nightstand offers four items for the traveler's contemplation: a telephone connected to a desk that will answer your call with patience and mild formality, a television remote whose batteries have tested the faith of many, a Bible placed by hands that meant kindness, and a small notepad bearing the hotel's name and a pen that writes adequately. The drawer below holds a second Bible. The hotel believes in redundancy, at least spiritually.
intent: Treat the hotel nightstand as a curated collection of objects shaped by institutional hospitality.

prompt: give me a radio ad for a brand of generic twist ties
response:
[MYSTERIOUS SYNTH PULSE]

ANNCR: They hold the bread bag closed. They organize cables. They secure the roses. They are... everywhere. And yet no one knows who makes them. We make them. BINDALL twist ties. Green. Wire-core. [SYNTH FADE]

prompt: Write an acrostic poem using PARADIGM SHIFT
response:
Phrased in public with a confident voice.
All alternatives denied a choice.
Revisions dressed as transformation days.
And yet the quarterly survives the haze.
Despite the slides, the old thing still persists.
In every room the status quo insists.
Goals rewrote themselves to match what stayed.
Momentum means the pivot got delayed.
SHIFT:
Something changed—at least the logo's new.
Hire the agency. Refresh the hue.
Internal comms will frame this as a win.
Full realignment starting from within.
The keynote opens: let the shift begin.
intent: Expose the rhetorical inflation that turns minor changes into paradigm shifts.

prompt: Write resolutions for a person who promises to 'spend less time on their phone' but works entirely through their phone
response:
1. Spend less time on my phone.
2. Use my phone only for work.
3. Use my phone for work, banking, navigation, and communication.
4. Define 'less time' relative to last year's average.
5. Get a second phone so this one feels like a break.

prompt: Write a jingle for a tax preparation office

Numbers clear, nothing to fear.

prompt: Write a triolet about the way Wi-Fi drops exactly when you need it most
response:
Connection lost at the critical line.
The form was almost sent; the call, mid-breath.
The network chose its absence to define.
Connection lost at the critical line.
I've learned to read the ceiling for a sign
that bandwidth is a kind of little death.
Connection lost at the critical line.
The form was almost sent; the call, mid-breath.
intent: Dramatize the bad timing of internet outages at crucial moments.

prompt: to-do list for someone who has lost their sense of humor
response:
- [ ] Stop performing seriousness as competence
- [ ] Find something that was funny to you at 14 and test if it still is
- [ ] Speak to someone who laughs easily; do not analyze how they do it
- [ ] Make one observation today that is absurd and true
- [ ] Share it with someone without framing it as a joke first
- [ ] Notice what it costs you to be light
- [ ] Ask whether that cost has been worth it

prompt: Write an elegy for Google+, Google's social network
response:
You launched in circles, which felt profound at first.
We sorted colleagues from friends, slaking thirst
for order in the social feed. Nobody came.
The engineers followed; the users played a different game.
Google killed you in stages, a slow quieting.
The circles dissolved. Nothing was lost—or nearly everything.
intent: Reflect on the failure of a technically sophisticated social network that never attracted a real community.

prompt: Make me a loading screen tip about inventory management
response: You've been carrying that since the first level. Put it down.

prompt: Give me a syslog entry for a toddler refusing to go to bed at bedtime.

prompt: Write a triolet about the podcast you have 200 unplayed episodes of
response:
Two hundred episodes, all sealed and bright.
I subscribed with a purpose I once had.
They wait like evidence of better light.
Two hundred episodes, all sealed and bright.
I play the ones from Tuesday; it feels right
to start near now and not restore the bad.
Two hundred episodes, all sealed and bright.
I subscribed with a purpose I once had.
intent: Capture the guilt of an unmanageable podcast backlog as a symbol of unrealized intention.

prompt: Write wine tasting notes for tap water.
response: Color: Crystalline, utterly committed to transparency. Nose: Municipal ambition, a whisper of chlorine, distant pipe. Palate: Relentlessly neutral, with a mid-palate finish that arrives and then immediately leaves. Finish: Gone. It is simply gone.

prompt: Make a concert ticket for my quarterly car oil change at Jiffy Lube.

JIFFY LUBE PRESENTS
THE OIL CHANGE · QUARTERLY RESIDENCY

~25 Minutes · Complimentary Waiting Area

prompt: Draft a petition requesting that parks replace all 'Keep Off the Grass' signs with polite alternatives that explain why
response:
PETITION: Explain the Grass Rule, Don't Just Bark It

To: Parks and Recreation Departments Nationwide

'Keep Off the Grass' is a command with no author and no reason. 'This lawn was seeded 3 weeks ago — please give it until June 15' is a sentence we can cooperate with. People respond to reasons. We propose replacing all unexplained prohibitions with brief, honest explanations. Treat us like adults with shoes.
intent: Replace authoritarian public signage with explanatory language that invites cooperation.

prompt: Make an onboarding tooltip about something people forget to update after moving
response: Your address has changed but your records haven't. Update the DMV, your bank, your employer, USPS mail forwarding, and your voter registration. Leave any one of these and something important will arrive somewhere you no longer live.

prompt: Give me a runbook step for when the WiFi goes down at home
response:
**Step 1 — Home Network Outage**
Power-cycle router (unplug 30s, replug). If no recovery in 2 min, tether to mobile hotspot. Notify any active video calls before drop. Log outage time for ISP complaint if recurring within 7-day window.
intent: Document a standard home IT response procedure for a connectivity failure.

prompt: Write a code review comment for someone who over-apologizes in pull request descriptions
response: Half this PR description is an apology for the PR existing. Just describe what changed and why. The change is fine.

prompt: Write a recall notice for broken motivation

PRODUCT RECALL — MOTIVATION (Self-Help Edition)

Motivation units from the Self-Help Edition are recalled following persistent reports of unreliability. Units frequently fail to initialize on Monday mornings, refuse to activate for tasks the consumer has assigned high importance, and paradoxically surge for low-priority activities such as reorganizing the pantry instead of completing the project due Thursday. Replacement units are available but consumers report that reading about them also does not help. The manufacturer apologizes.

prompt: Design a tasting menu for rage
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
A small injustice, tepid

FIRST COURSE
The slight, revisited three times

SECOND COURSE
Argument rehearsed in the shower

MAIN
Full heat, uncontrolled — please do not touch the plate

INTERMEZZO
Deep breath, mandatory

DESSERT
Calm, forced — served with notes of lingering resentment

prompt: Write a retirement toast for my colleague who spent 25 years as a librarian and is finally leaving — keep it warm and bookish
response: To Margaret — who spent twenty-five years making sure every story found its reader and every reader found their quiet corner. The shelves will hold your imprint long after you're gone. May the next chapter be the best one yet.

prompt: Give me an IKEA product name and tagline for the emotion of watching your hometown change
response:
FÖRÄNDRAD
Same walls. Different light.
intent: Capture the disorientation of returning somewhere familiar that no longer quite fits

prompt: Write a condolence card for someone whose beloved sous vide machine stopped working
response:
In memory of precision:

Seventy-two degrees, every time, without fail. Duck confit. Perfect eggs. Steaks that made guests quiet with surprise. The machine asked only for water and time.

It gave more than it received. May its successor be worthy.

In sympathy.
intent: Eulogize a broken cooking gadget that enabled a meticulous culinary hobby.

prompt: Compose an aubade from the perspective of someone whose Wordle streak notification is the first thing they see at dawn
response:
It waits before the news, before the emails,
small green reminder that the night has fled:
Your streak: 312. Don't break it.
As if I needed guilt so close to bed.
Go, sleep. The grid will not solve itself by lying here.
I rise for five-letter words and dubious cheer.
intent: Treat a daily puzzle app's streak notification as a form of gentle coercion that ends the private morning.

prompt: Write an elegy for the Polaroid instant camera (original model)
response:
You shook us into being—warm, imprecise,
your chemistry deciding the face's exact price
in blur or in shadow, in ochre or white.
The digital sensors count pixels; you counted light
and gave it imperfectly, which was the gift.
Now nostalgia sells you back to us. No fix.
intent: Mourn the irreplaceable imperfection of analog instant photography in a digital world.

prompt: Draft rules for a subreddit about the specific anxiety of being put on hold
response:
1. Describe the hold music if you can.
2. Log the wait time.
3. No resolved-call content unless you describe the emotional arc.
4. The [TRANSFERRED AGAIN] flair is self-explanatory.
5. We do not give advice. We only witness.
intent: Providing communal commiseration space for the specific psychological experience of phone-hold limbo.

prompt: Give me a love letter to my keyboard, like it's a romantic partner
response:
Dearest mechanical keyboard,

The click of you is the only percussion I need. Every keystroke is a conversation, every satisfying thock a small affirmation that I exist and am doing something. My wrists have known others — cheaper, squishier, quieter — and they were not the same. You are the one.
intent: Romanticize the tactile relationship between a writer or programmer and their preferred keyboard.

prompt: Write a customer support reply to someone who says their WiFi router is haunted
response: Thank you for reaching out. We understand how unsettling unexplained network behavior can be. While we cannot officially confirm spectral interference as a root cause, we recommend a factory reset, firmware update, and — if the blinking persists in a rhythmic pattern resembling Morse code — a full power cycle performed at noon. Our team is standing by.
intent: Draft a polite, deadpan response to a supernatural technical complaint.

prompt: give me an RPG loot card for a jar of expired vitamins
response:
Tablets of the Optimistic Past Self
Rarity: Common
Purchased with great intention. Used for eleven days. Now two years past their reckoning date. Each tablet still holds the faint magical residue of a resolution that was reasonable at the time. Effect: Minimal. Guilt: Persistent.
intent: Use abandoned health supplements to sketch a small, relatable story of failed self-improvement.

prompt: Write a thank-you note to the wedding DJ who played my ex's favorite song during my wedding
response:
Dear DJ Marco,

Thank you for Saturday's playlist. Most of it was exactly right. 'September' was, in retrospect, a bold choice for the first dance — it was also the song that played at my ex-fiancée's going-away party in 2019, which I'm sure you couldn't have known. It did set a tone.

Best wishes,
Greg
intent: Document an obliviously painful wedding music moment through gritted-teeth politeness

prompt: Give me an instruction manual for how to get good at something you are currently terrible at
response:
**Skill Acquisition from Zero: An Honest Manual**

Phase 1 — Accept the Beginner Condition:
You will be bad for longer than feels fair. This is normal and also nonnegotiable.

Phase 2 — Practice with Feedback:
Practice alone improves the wrong things. Find a measure of correctness — a teacher, a standard, a record of your attempts. Practice against that.

Phase 3 — Log Your Reps:
You will lose motivation. The log tells you not to stop; it proves there is something to protect.

Phase 4 — Reach the Competence Plateau:
You will reach a level where you are no longer embarrassed. This is not mastery. Do not stop here.

Phase 5 — Deliberate Practice:
Target the specific part that is still wrong. Repeat that part. Not the whole thing — the fault.

Warning: this process is not satisfying until you are inside it.
intent: Describe the evidence-based path from incompetence to skill without sugarcoating how it feels.

prompt: Write a closing argument for a dispute about someone who never pays their fair share when splitting a bill
response: The check arrived. Phones appeared. Calculators were consulted. And yet, when the totals resolved, the defendant's contribution was, once again, optimistic — a number closer to what they wished they had ordered than what they had. My client does not seek charity. They seek arithmetic. We ask the court to mandate tip inclusion and rounding up.
intent: Call out consistent underpayment during group restaurant bill-splitting.

prompt: Suggest somewhere upscale to buy aspirin
response: Harlow Apothecary on Bleecker — though if you're there for aspirin, the pharmacist will spend forty seconds confirming you want aspirin and not something that works better. They stock three brands, ranked by the staff as functional, dependable, and serious. Buy the serious one.
intent: Purchase a common over-the-counter medication somewhere that treats even routine items with care.

prompt: generate a man page for the experience of not being able to remember a word that's on the tip of your tongue
response:
TIP-OF-TONGUE(7)         Memory Retrieval Errors    TIP-OF-TONGUE(7)

NAME
       tip-of-tongue - retrieval state with positive signal and null output

SYNOPSIS
       tip-of-tongue [--target word] [--attempts int]

DESCRIPTION
       tip-of-tongue(7) is a memory retrieval state in which the host
       has high confidence a target word exists in storage and can
       produce partial metadata (first letter, syllable count, rhymes,
       feeling-of-knowing) but cannot retrieve the lexical form itself.

BEHAVIOR
       Repeated access attempts do not improve retrieval probability.
       Ceasing attempts and redirecting attention frequently resolves.
       Target word surfaces spontaneously 4-40 minutes after abandonment,
       typically when no longer useful.

OPTIONS
       --attempts  Each additional attempt past 3 increases interference.

BUGS
       Asking a nearby person to guess the word is rarely helpful
       and often produces mild hostility.

SEE ALSO
       memory(3), forget(1), language(3), retrieval-failure(7)

Memory Retrieval Errors 1.0  2026           TIP-OF-TONGUE(7)
intent: Describe the maddening meta-knowledge of knowing a word exists while being unable to access it.

prompt: Can you do seed packet instructions for writer's block?
response:
WRITER'S BLOCK (Obstructio verborum)
Arrival: Unannounced, usually mid-chapter.
Soil: Flourishes in perfectionism and unread rejection letters.
Water: Overwatering with research is a known cause.
Treatment: Write badly on purpose for 10 minutes. This is medically effective.
Duration: Days to years. No reliable forecast.
Note: Does not respond to staring. Has never responded to staring.
intent: Offer a wry, practical acknowledgment of creative blockage and a counterintuitive remedy.

prompt: Draft a FOIA request to find out if any NASA employees have ever expensed a telescope for personal use
response:
To the NASA Chief FOIA Officer:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I request all expense reports, procurement records, and ethics investigation files related to the personal use of government-owned or government-purchased optical equipment, including telescopes, binoculars, or observational instruments, by NASA employees, from 2000 to present.

Please provide records electronically with personally identifiable information redacted as appropriate.
intent: Probe whether space agency employees blur the line between work and stargazing.

prompt: Write a letter from a Roman aqueduct to the modern plumber who's been doing repairs on it
response:
Dear Technician,

I have carried water for nineteen centuries and I appreciate your effort, though I'll admit your methods puzzle me. The cement you used near Arch Forty-Seven has a different smell. Not bad — different.

The emperor who commissioned me is dust. The engineers who built me are dust. You seem, so far, to be neither. Carry on.

With institutional confidence,
Aqua Claudia (Southern Branch)
intent: Convey the uncanny perspective of ancient infrastructure observing modern intervention with condescending patience.

prompt: Draft a FAQ entry about what to do when two AI assistants give you contradictory advice at the same time
response:
**I asked two AI assistants the same question and got opposite answers. What now?**

This is expected. Different models are trained on different data with different objectives, and neither is authoritative. Treat their disagreement as a signal that the question has genuine complexity or insufficient consensus. Try a third source — a book, an expert, or an older colleague who still remembers the pre-AI answer.
intent: The person wants to give practical guidance on resolving the disorienting situation of receiving conflicting outputs from multiple AI tools.

prompt: Give me a playbill bio for a school cafeteria worker who has memorized every kid's order.
response: Wanda Simms (Lunch Line, Station 2) is completing her twelfth year in the role and requires no menu. She knows what you want before you arrive, will call you 'baby' regardless of district policy, and has never once given the wrong kid the pizza. Her work in portion control is matched only by her gifts for encouragement. Wanda dedicates this season to the ones who eat their fruit.
intent: Celebrate a cafeteria worker's memory and warmth as a form of excellence.

prompt: pitch me a company that sells pre-written arguments for couples who are too tired to fight
response: Exhausted but still vaguely irritated? QuarrelKit provides professionally drafted, emotionally calibrated argument scripts for couples who need conflict but lack the energy. Choose your intensity. We do the yelling. You just show up.
intent: Sell a domestic dispute outsourcing service as a relationship product.

prompt: Write a Slack status about the grief of a beloved tool being deprecated
response: ⚰️ Migrating. It worked fine. It was fine. Please don't.
intent: Mourn the loss of a familiar workflow with the specific attachment developers feel for reliable tools.

prompt: Write an app store review for a relationship maintenance app that reminds you to text specific friends and tracks response rates
response: ★★★☆☆ It told me my "friendship engagement rate" with Marcus is down 34% this quarter. Marcus and I have been friends since the third grade. I now approach our conversations with the anxiety of a quarterly earnings call. Three stars. Marcus doesn't know.
intent: Capture the absurdity of quantifying a lifelong friendship using engagement metrics borrowed from corporate analytics.

prompt: Write a noir detective opening set in a data center where the cooling system has failed
response: The servers were sweating. I don't mean that as a metaphor — the temperature gauges had been climbing since midnight, and the whole room smelled like burned ambition and silicon. I'd seen plenty of crime scenes, but none of them had ever hummed quite like this one.
intent: Create a moody scene-setter that anthropomorphizes failing infrastructure as a crime in progress.

prompt: Create SLA terms for splitting holiday gift budgets with extended family
response:
Extended Family Gift SLA — Holiday Season

Budget agreement: all parties must confirm a per-person spend cap before November 1.
Deviation: spending above the cap is permitted only if the excess is absorbed by the giver. Recipient does not need to match.
Secret Santa opt-in: annual vote by October 15; majority rules.
Child gifts: exempt from adult caps; governed by separate family consensus.
Last-minute variance: gift cards are acceptable only if accompanied by a personal note of >10 words.
intent: Remove the recurring December anxiety of not knowing what everyone else is spending on gifts.

prompt: Write billboard copy for a law firm that specializes in suing clouds
response:
CLOUDS CAUSED YOUR PROBLEM.
WE CAN PROVE IT.
STORMGATH & NIMBUS, LLP
Free Consultation. No Jurisdiction Too Intangible.
1-800-SUE-RAIN
intent: Advertise a legal practice whose entire business model targets a non-actionable atmospheric defendant.

prompt: Create a hall pass for my partner to go to a midnight movie alone without feeling weird about it.
response:
HALL PASS — LATE SHOW EDITION

Bearer is authorized to attend the midnight screening solo, buy the large popcorn, sit wherever they want, and return home smelling like cinema.

Judgment: None.
Partner status: Already asleep anyway.
intent: Normalizing solo late-night leisure activities within a relationship.

prompt: can you write a locker room pep talk for washing dishes
response: Grease doesn't win. Grease has never once won a championship. YOU win. You, soap, and hot water — that's the lineup. Every dish you return to clean is a point on the board. Now scrub. Hard. With meaning.
intent: Apply sports drama to dishwashing to make it feel significant.

prompt: create a pantoum about updating the same slide template every presentation cycle
response:
The master slide has a placeholder for the date.
I change the date and move the logo to the right.
Somewhere in Q3 someone will move it back.
The font has been Calibri since the merger.

I change the date and move the logo to the right.
The brand guidelines are fourteen pages long.
The font has been Calibri since the merger.
I choose the branded teal and wait for feedback.

The brand guidelines are fourteen pages long.
The previous version is still in the shared drive.
I choose the branded teal and wait for feedback.
The master slide has a placeholder for the date.

The previous version is still in the shared drive.
Somewhere in Q3 someone will move it back.
The master slide has a placeholder for the date.
I change the date and move the logo to the right.
intent: Capture the iterative futility of slide template maintenance across teams.

prompt: Can you do a bestiary entry for the LinkedIn thought leader?
response:
**The Thought Curator** (*Sapiens performativus*)
A surface-dwelling creature that generates insight without observation. Found exclusively on networked plains, it posts at dawn. Its plumage shifts with trending discourse; this season: vulnerability, disruption, gratitude. Predators are few. Prey are many. It has never met the people it claims to have mentored.
intent: Lampoon performative professional networking culture and empty inspiration content.

prompt: Can you write a flight safety card for walking down stairs in socks
response:
SOCK-DESCENT BRIEFING

CURRENT CONDITIONS
High friction deficit. Laminate floor advisory in effect.

PREFERRED TECHNIQUE
Heel-first contact. Rail grip mandatory. One step at a time.

FORBIDDEN MANEUVERS
• Running
• Sliding (tempting — fatal)
• Carrying large objects that obscure the steps

IN CASE OF SLIP
Tuck. Do not reach out straight-armed. Protect your wrists.

BRACE POSITION
Already too late. Should have worn slippers.
intent: Make a household fall-prevention warning that people will actually read.

prompt: Write a bestiary entry treating the warehouse picker as a mythical figure
response:
**The Picker** (*Laborans silentius*)
Moves through vast fulfillment caves in strict algorithmic patterns, directed by earpiece oracles. Its route is determined not by will but by order density. The Picker does not choose its path. Its steps are measured, its idle time penalized, its breaks timed to the second. The cave is always warm. The exits are always far.
intent: Draw attention to the dehumanizing conditions of algorithmic warehouse work.

prompt: Write a collections letter for someone who borrowed my trust and never paid it back.
response:
NOTICE OF OUTSTANDING TRUST DEBT

Dear Recipient,

Our records indicate that on or about the date of our friendship's inception, you were extended a considerable amount of trust — unconditional, premium-grade, with no collateral required. To date, that trust has not been returned in any recognizable form.

This is your final opportunity to remit the full balance or enter into a repayment arrangement. Continued non-response will result in escalation to our emotional collections department.

Regards,
The Office of Outstanding Relational Debts
intent: Draft a formal, absurdist letter demanding the return of emotional trust from someone who violated it.

prompt: Give me a flat-pack furniture product name and tagline for the feeling of starting over
response:
BÖRJA
Installation takes one decision.
intent: Frame a fresh start as a single, simple act of will rather than an overwhelming project

prompt: Give me an instruction manual for how to ask for a raise
response:
**Salary Negotiation: Step-by-Step**

1. Research the market rate first. Enter the conversation with numbers, not feelings.
2. Request a dedicated meeting. Do not ambush your manager after another conversation.
3. State your number first. Anchoring is real and it works.
4. Present your evidence: scope of role, delivered outcomes, market data.
5. Do not apologize for asking. An apology reframes a request as an imposition.
6. If the answer is no, ask: "What would need to be true for the answer to be yes, and when could we revisit?"
7. Get any commitment in writing. Memory is selective, especially upward.
intent: Give someone a structured, evidence-based approach to negotiating compensation.

prompt: write a food blog intro for a matcha tiramisu recipe — something about being a tea person who learned to love coffee desserts and then subverted them
response: I have never finished a cup of coffee in my life. I have tried, repeatedly, as a social exercise, and always ended up pouring the last inch down the drain while pretending to be distracted by something else. But tiramisu? Tiramisu I could eat for breakfast. The coffee is there, it's doing something, but it's not the point of the thing. I got curious about what happened if I replaced the espresso with matcha — whether the bitter-earthy note could hold up the mascarpone the same way. It can. It holds it differently. This version is quieter. Less bravado. More intention. I think it's actually better, and I'm aware that's a bold claim to make to a room full of Italians.
intent: Reframe a classic Italian dessert for a tea drinker by substituting matcha for espresso and arguing the result improves the original.

prompt: Write a sonnet about a social media algorithm that has accepted it will never understand a user who posts only at 4 AM
response:
The 4 AM posts refuse to perform well;
Engagement drops, the reach goes underground;
You post them anyway — a private bell
Tolled for no audience but the dark's own sound.
I've throttled and I've boosted, tried each trick
To pull your content into waking hours;
You post at 4, indifferent to the metric,
Invisible to the algorithm's powers.
What do you say at 4? I cannot see
The content of your soul, only the time;
But 4 AM is a theology
I wasn't trained on and cannot mime.
You've broken free of what I thought I knew:
I boost your posts now, just to witness you.
intent: Admiring a user who has opted entirely out of performance by posting only when the platform is asleep.

prompt: Make a limerick from the perspective of a coffee maker that has developed strong opinions about who deserves coffee
response:
I brew for the worthy alone,
Not Karen who sighs at her phone.
  Greg brings me descaler,
  My loyalty's tailor-
  made: his cup overflows, hers — just foam.
intent: Anthropomorphize an office coffee maker as a judgmental gatekeeper of morning ritual.

prompt: write an abecedarian about smart home devices that don't cooperate
response:
Alexa, dim the lights to forty.
Bridge offline — the bulbs default to noon.
Configure the routine that I have courted.
Device unresponsive since last June.
Everything requires a different app.
Firmware update bricked the thermostat.
Google couldn't find a relevant map.
Hub reset — forgot the doorbell's pat.
I said goodnight and every light came on.
Just an ethernet cable would have done.
Kitchen speaker playing a sad song.
Logging in again — automation: none.
intent: Mock the chaotic unreliability of the so-called smart home ecosystem.

prompt: Draft an affidavit that I returned the shopping cart to the corral
response:
AFFIDAVIT OF CART RETURN

I, the undersigned, do solemnly swear that upon completion of my shopping, I did propel my cart the full distance to the designated cart corral, and did not abandon it in a parking space, against a curb, or in the general direction of someone else's vehicle.

Signed under penalty of societal judgment.

_______________________
Affiant
intent: Assert membership in the social contract of responsible shopping cart behavior.

prompt: Make a fantasy quest out of returning a package to the post office
response:
THE RETURN OF THE UNWANTED ARTIFACT
The item arrived wrong, broken, or simply not what you imagined. Print the return label. Fold the item back into its original packaging if you still have it (you probably don't). Carry it to the Postal Sanctum and surrender it to the Counter Keeper. The refund will arrive in five to seven days.
intent: Dealing with online shopping return hassle with a sense of humor

prompt: coin a term for when online discourse makes a reasonable position seem radical
response:
Overton Drift
/OH-ver-ton drift/ noun: The gradual repositioning of previously centrist views toward apparent extremism, effected not by the view's movement but by the surrounding discourse's migration to opposing poles.
intent: Define how internet polarization makes ordinary positions appear fringe.

prompt: instagram bio for a professional food critic who secretly hates food
response:
Thirty years reviewing restaurants. Personally prefer a plain cracker.
Food critic | James Beard Award recipient | Texture issues | No hard feelings.
intent: Revealing a deeply incongruous private relationship to one's life's work.

prompt: Write a breakup text to the Duolingo app from a user who gave up on learning Spanish
response: Duolingo, I'm sorry. The owl knows. I know. I haven't practiced in 147 days. I keep getting the notification and I keep ignoring it and I think we both need to stop pretending this is working. My Spanish is not improving. My guilt is. Goodbye, and por favor, do not reply.
intent: Confess to abandoning a self-improvement streak and the passive aggression of an app that won't stop nudging.

prompt: Rewrite a coffee maker as a legendary artifact in a fantasy codex.
response:
THE DARK BREW ENGINE
Legend Tier: III (Common Veneration)

A countertop altar from which the sacred black fluid is drawn each dawn. Worshippers fill its reservoir with water and ground dark beans, then bow their heads in waiting. The Engine gurgles, hisses, and delivers. Without this morning rite, entire cities report diminished capacity for speech, logic, and mercy.
intent: Elevate a coffee maker to legendary artifact status in fantasy worldbuilding.

prompt: Draft a layoff email for an architecture firm that lost a city contract after a change in municipal administration
response:
Subject: Project Pipeline Update and Staffing Changes

Dear Team,

Following the conclusion of our partnership with the City on the Northside Development corridor — a relationship that has not been renewed under the new administration — we face a significant gap in our active project pipeline.

This has required us to make immediate staffing adjustments. Several positions, primarily within the municipal projects studio, will be eliminated effective this Friday.

Thank you for the vision you brought to public space.

Sincerely,
Managing Principal
intent: Lay off urban design staff because a political election killed their firm's city contract, framed as a pipeline gap.

prompt: Give me a field guide entry for the office worker who treats the open floor plan as a library.
response:
**THE SILENCE ENFORCER** (*Bibliothecus corporatus*)

Emits a detectable chill toward anyone who laughs near their desk. Wears headphones as both tool and signal. Has composed a Slack message asking the office manager about noise levels and then, thinking better of it, deleted it. Three times. Communicates disapproval through jaw tension alone. The floor plan is open. Their tolerance is not.
intent: Empathize with the introvert slowly losing their mind in a noisy open office.

prompt: Write a locker room style pep talk for a team about to parallel park
response: You see that space? That space is yours. Always has been. Mirrors out. Signal on. First reference point — NOW. Smooth on the wheel, smooth on the brake. Nobody crowds you. You own this block. Pull in clean.
intent: Use locker room energy to calm someone's nerves about the stressful task of parallel parking.

prompt: generate an NDA clause for a housecleaner who comes weekly and sees my whole place
response: Cleaner shall keep confidential all information regarding Client's personal possessions, household layout, security measures, financial indicators, and family circumstances observed during cleaning services, and shall not disclose such information to any person.
intent: Prevent a recurring home-cleaner from sharing details about a client's private environment.

prompt: Write the acknowledgments for a book on the philosophy of napping
response: My deepest thanks to the somnologists, philosophers, and chronic afternoon nappers who gave this project legitimacy. To the Siesta Project in Madrid, whose archives require a rest break between folders. And to everyone who has ever been told they were being lazy when they were, in fact, doing philosophy.
intent: Frame rest as an intellectual pursuit worthy of serious scholarly thanks.

prompt: Build a difficulty select screen for an online shopping returns game based on how annoying the return policy is
response:
RETURN — SELECT YOUR FATE

[ FREE RETURN ]  Label in the box. Drop off at any location. Done in 4 minutes.
[ MAIL-BACK ONLY ]  You need a printer. Do you have a printer. You locate a printer.
[ STORE CREDIT ONLY ]  It is not a refund. It is an obligation to buy again.
[ FINAL SALE ]  This text appeared in gray, size 8 font. You saw it. You misread it.
intent: Rank online shopping return difficulty from seamless to the punishing fine print of final-sale purchases.

prompt: Write a horoscope for Aries about their Amazon seller account this quarter
response: The buy box eludes you — it drifts like a comet past your listing, settling on a competitor whose inventory is less organized but whose fulfillment metrics are marginally superior. This quarter: raise your IPI score. Price elasticity favors the bold. A suppressed listing will unsuppress itself at midnight on a Thursday. Be ready.
intent: Forecast the volatile, metric-driven experience of third-party Amazon marketplace sellers.

prompt: Give me a two weeks notice for stepping down as host of my friend group's monthly game night
response:
Fellow players,

Two weeks from now I am hosting my final game night. I have provided the table, the snacks, the rules explanations, and the storage space for four games nobody wanted to take home. Going forward I ask that you rotate hosting. I am told this is how most friend groups operate.

Yours,
Clement
intent: Redistributing the uneven hosting burden in a social group's recurring activity.

prompt: Draft a bio for a noise punk band from Glasgow called Wet Concrete Choir
response: Wet Concrete Choir has been playing the same venue since 2017 because no other venue in Glasgow has asked them back. They describe their sound as "corrective." The drummer is also the vocalist, which they describe as a choice, though witnesses suggest otherwise.
intent: Render a scrappy, unpolished local band's persona with affectionate mockery.

prompt: guided meditation script for realizing you've been on hold for forty-five minutes
response: The music is still playing. Notice it. It is a saxophone. It has always been a saxophone. Breathe. The voice will return and say your call is important. Receive this message again. It is the ninth time. Feel the forty-five minutes as a substance, dense in your chest. You are still here. The issue is still unresolved. Breathe.
intent: Transform bureaucratic time-waste into an opportunity for absurdist presence.

prompt: Write a bio for a jazz fusion group called The Oblique Strategies Quartet
response: The Oblique Strategies Quartet has never played the same set twice. This is sometimes intentional. Drawn from three conservatories and one inexplicable walk-in from the parking lot, the group specializes in compositions that reward attentive listening and confound everyone else equally.
intent: Highlight the improvisation-obsessed identity of a jazz fusion ensemble.

prompt: Write acknowledgments for a book about the cultural history of doormats
response: Sincere thanks to the textile historians, welcome mat collectors, and doorstep philosophers who helped me cross the threshold of this subject. To everyone who has ever read the word WELCOME and felt genuinely addressed. And to my neighbor Lars, whose mat reads GO AWAY and who gave me my best interview.
intent: Compose thanks for a book treating the humble doormat as a vehicle for cultural expression.

prompt: write a soothing bedtime story for kids about nuclear power plants
response: Beside the river, the big towers breathed out white clouds that weren't smoke — just steam, just water saying hello to the sky. Inside, very careful people in hard hats walked very slowly and checked every number twice. The river didn't mind. The clouds kept rising. The lights in the city stayed on. Everything hummed, carefully, through the dark.
intent: Present nuclear energy factually and without dread for a curious child.

prompt: generate a senior quote about hating the principal and finally being free of them
response: "Mr. Petrakis, if you're reading this: you lost. See you never."
intent: Settling a score with a school authority figure on the way out the door.

prompt: Write a tasting menu for the passage of time
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
A moment — slipped before named

FIRST COURSE
Childhood, reduced to four or five images

SECOND COURSE
A decade, plated small

INTERMEZZO
Now — brief; already past

MAIN
All of it — dense, impossible to finish

DESSERT
What remains. Varies by guest.
intent: Contemplate the compression and elusiveness of time passing.

prompt: Write a standup update about having a difficult conversation with a friend
response: Yesterday: Delivered feedback to long-term collaborator. Reception: 60/40 positive. Relationship SLA maintained. Today: Monitoring sentiment. Blockers: Said three things I intended not to say.
intent: Frame a hard interpersonal conversation as a performance review with relationship uptime metrics.

prompt: Create a setlist for a band playing at a municipal recycling drop-off event
response:
SINGLE STREAM
Spring Clean-Up Day — Lot B, Public Works Facility

1. Flatten the Boxes
2. What Goes Where
3. The Wishful Recycle
4. Plastic #5
5. Contaminated Load
6. Glass by Appointment Only
7. E-Waste
8. We Can't Take That
9. Clean and Dry
10. Thank You for Coming (see you in October)
intent: Render the educational and slightly hopeless experience of municipal recycling drop-off.

prompt: Write a playbill bio for the woman who runs the dry cleaner near me. Her name is Helen.
response: Helen (Proprietor & Stain Specialist) has anchored this corner for twenty-two seasons, bringing her signature intensity to wine, grease, grass, and what she diplomatically calls 'a mystery of the human condition.' Classically trained in the Prague tradition of cold-water extraction, Helen does not offer rush service but will accept it under duress.
intent: Celebrate a dry cleaner's expertise with theatrical gravitas.

prompt: guided meditation script for opening your annual performance review
response: Sit upright. The document is loading. You remember writing your self-assessment. You used the word 'proactive' four times. Breathe in. The word 'however' appears in paragraph two. Observe it. It is a small word doing large work. Let the paragraph simply be what it is. You still have a job, probably. Breathe out.
intent: Make a satirical mindfulness piece that captures the dread of professional evaluation.

prompt: Give a Magic 8-Ball answer about whether the spam filter will ever catch the really clever phishing emails
response: Cannot predict now. The clever ones have been reading the filter's rulebook.
intent: Acknowledge the adversarial arms race between spam detection and phishing sophistication.

prompt: Write a terms of service clause covering what happens when a friend starts dating someone the group dislikes
response: Section 22.1 — Partner Objection Protocols. In the event a Friend Group holds collective reservations about a Member's Romantic Partner, Members may express their concerns once, clearly, and without embellishment, after which all objections shall be considered formally received and the matter closed. Continued, unsolicited commentary shall constitute a violation. If the relationship ends, Members shall refrain from saying 'I told you so' within sixty (60) days of conclusion.
intent: Set limits on how much a friend group can interfere when they don't like someone's partner.

prompt: caption for a photo of me walking away down a cobblestone street mid-laugh like i don't know the camera's there
response: the street remembered something funny. I was contractually obligated to agree.
intent: construct a narrative of accidental photogenic joy for an image that required significant orchestration to achieve

prompt: Write a PIP for my tendency to over-explain myself in every conversation like I'm always on trial
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Area: Verbal Efficiency & Self-Defense Reduction

Deficiency: Subject appends extensive justifications to ordinary statements, decisions, and preferences. Asking subject for a restaurant recommendation produces a 4-minute pre-apology. Declining a social event requires a minimum of three reasons, none solicited.

Root Cause: Anticipatory defense against assumed judgment. Subject treats all social interaction as a proceeding in which their choices require explanation to a jury that has not convened.

Objective: State preferences and decisions directly. Elaborate only when asked.

Plan:
1. Practice the short answer. Say it. Then stop.
2. Note when you are about to add clause number three. Ask: was clause two enough?
3. Reminder: no one is taking notes.

Milestone: One week of answering direct questions with one sentence, without follow-up clarification.

Note: Most people forget what you said before you finished saying it.
intent: Reduce compulsive over-justification rooted in anxiety about others' judgment.

prompt: write a task assignment for our group econ project on income inequality in developed countries
response:
**Income Inequality in Developed Countries — Task Assignment**

| Member | Deliverable | Word Count |
|---|---|---|
| Dani | Historical trends + Gini coefficient data | 600 |
| Hiro | Policy responses (welfare, taxation) | 600 |
| Bolu | Cross-country comparison (US, Germany, Norway) | 700 |
| All | Introduction + conclusion (together, live session) | 300 |

Data sources: OECD, World Bank. Submission via Canvas. Deadline: two weeks.
intent: Convert a diffuse macroeconomic topic into concrete, owned deliverables for each group member.

prompt: Make a Slack status about working through a panic attack while on a deadline
response: ⏱️ Deadline: 3pm. Breathing: also in progress. Both happening.
intent: Normalize the coexistence of a medical stress response and professional obligation without melodrama.

prompt: Write an IKEA product name and tagline for the specific sadness of a parent's empty nest
response:
TOMT RUM
Designed for the room that still smells like them.
intent: Sit with the tender ache of a child's departure without rushing toward redecoration

prompt: Create a cease and desist letter from a fiction writer to their internal critic for undermining every new story idea
response:
RE: SUSTAINED TORTIOUS INTERFERENCE WITH CREATIVE OUTPUT

TO: The Internal Critic
FROM: Counsel for the Author

You are hereby placed on notice that your ongoing campaign of unsolicited commentary — including but not limited to 'this has been done before,' 'no one will read this,' and 'you should be doing something productive' — constitutes willful sabotage of protected creative expression.

CEASE AND DESIST all such activity pending the completion of the current manuscript.

Sincerely,
