Ketchup packet reserves at the Milldale County Fair have dropped below acceptable levels. Mustard supplies remain stable. Relish is unconfirmed. Corn dog and fry vendors are on limited condiment protocol. Please use sparingly. This is a fragile situation.
intent: Apply emergency management language to a trivial condiment shortage at a local fair.

prompt: Write a CAPTCHA instruction about the experience of choosing a checkout lane that turns out to be slower
response: Select which line you would have chosen. All lines are the wrong line. Your willingness to choose despite this is the verification.
intent: Use the futile optimism of lane selection in queues as a metaphor for human agency within rigged systems.

prompt: Write me SLA terms for keeping secrets in a friendship
response:
Confidentiality SLA — Friend-to-Friend

Information shared under "don't tell anyone": classified indefinitely unless explicitly declassified by originating party.
Self-disclosure clause: information about the listener themselves may be shared with their own therapist only.
Breaches: any unauthorized disclosure triggers mandatory acknowledgment, apology, and a moratorium on receiving new secrets for 90 days.
Expiry: secrets older than 10 years may be discussed with mutual friends, but never strangers.
intent: Formalize the sacred social contract around secret-keeping between close friends.

prompt: Write a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for managing your inbox without drowning in it
response:
INBOX TRIAGE KEYS

Ctrl+A — Do not select all and delete; this is a fantasy
Ctrl+F — Find anything actionable; move it to a task system
Esc — Stop reading email as it arrives; batch twice daily
Alt+F4 — Archive after reading if no action required
Ctrl+S — Save drafts; do not let them become buried sent items
Ctrl+Z — Undo the impulsive reply-all before it sends
Shift+Enter — Respond briefly; three sentences is enough
Ctrl+H — Replace inbox as default tab with something that isn't
intent: Help someone stop treating their email inbox as both a to-do list and an anxiety machine.

prompt: Write a calendar event description for a recurring customer escalation review where the same customers escalate every month.
response: Escalation Review — The list is familiar. Three accounts appear every month. We know their names. They know ours. Mitigation plans will be reviewed; some will have been updated. Next steps will be assigned. We will see you here again in 30 days.
intent: Convey the repetitive futility of reviewing customer problems that never actually get solved.

prompt: Give me an onboarding tooltip about renter's insurance
response: Your landlord's insurance covers the building. Not your laptop. Not your bike. Not your sleep. Renter's insurance covers your stuff and costs roughly the price of two lattes a month. Get it before you need it.
intent: Convince a new renter to purchase renter's insurance before something bad happens.

prompt: Write a will bequeathing my houseplant's unearned confidence to my anxious friend.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave the unearned confidence of my pothos, Gerald — who has survived drought, a broken window, and eighteen months facing the wrong direction — to my friend, Simone. She does not yet believe she is unkillable. Gerald always knew he was.
intent: Offer quiet encouragement to an anxious person by anthropomorphizing a resilient plant.

prompt: Can you write meeting minutes for a town hall Q&A where every question was answered with 'we're still working on that'?
response:
ALL-HANDS TOWN HALL — May 29, 2026
Presenter: CEO | Attendees: ~200

Questions submitted: 14
Questions answered definitively: 0

Representative exchange:
Q: What's the layoff plan?
A: We're still working on that.

Q: When does the new system go live?
A: We're still working on that.

Sentiment survey: Pending.
intent: Capture leadership's systematic evasion of direct employee concerns.

prompt: Write a setlist for a band called The Holdouts performing at a gym on January 7th
response:
THE HOLDOUTS
Live at Peak Performance Fitness — January 7

1. Still Here
2. The Resolution
3. The Parking Lot (packed)

5. New Shoes
6. We Were All New Once
7. The Crowded Week

intent: Capture the tension between gym regulars and New Year's resolution newcomers through a setlist.

prompt: Make a parking ticket for someone who sends calendar invites with zero context in the description field.
response:
CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: Reserved Spot Seized — Time Held Without Explanation
OFFENSE: Subject blocked 60 minutes of respondent's calendar under the title "Sync." No agenda. No description. No pre-read. Attendee arrived unprepared and remained so.
FINE: $65 or two bullet points in the body field
NOTE: "Sync" is a verb, not a plan.
intent: Call out the workplace habit of scheduling vague meetings with no preparation materials.

prompt: Write a dark, witty noir opener about a warehouse picker who realizes the items on her list don't add up
response: Picking two thousand units a shift, you stop reading the labels after the first week. It's all SKUs and barcodes and the scanner either beeps green or it doesn't. Mine beeped green every time. That was the problem. Some of those items weren't in the inventory system at all.
intent: Surface algorithmic blind spots in warehouse logistics as a cover for something being smuggled through fulfillment.

prompt: Headnote for black bean tacos about the first meal someone cooked for themselves after moving out at 18
response: I moved out at eighteen with forty dollars and a box of things that didn't belong together. The first meal I made in my own kitchen was black bean tacos because they were cheap, because I'd watched my mother make them enough times to approximate, and because I needed to eat something I had made myself in a space that was only mine. They were fine. I was ecstatic.
intent: Mark the emotional significance of independence through the simplest possible meal

prompt: Draft wedding vows from a person to their GPS app
response: I promise to follow your directions even when I am certain you are wrong. I will make the U-turn. I will take the toll road you recommend at midnight. I vow to trust you over my own instincts, which have failed me in the parking structures of this city more times than I can say. When you say recalculating, I will not curse your name. I will wait. I am yours.
intent: Capture the helpless trust and occasional frustration a driver feels toward navigation software.

prompt: Write a ToS clause about a friend who gives relationship advice despite being perpetually single
response: Section 21.7 — Qualifying Conditions for Advisory Input. Relational counsel may be offered by any User regardless of current partnership status, provided the advising party acknowledges the advisory is theoretical in nature. Advisees retain the right to weigh input accordingly. Neither party shall invoke the advisor's relationship history to invalidate their counsel; however, the advisor shall refrain from prefacing advice with 'I've never experienced this personally, but obviously...'
intent: Humorously formalize who is and isn't qualified to give relationship advice.

prompt: write a short apology note for sending a very long voice memo to someone instead of a text
response:
Hi Dara,

Sorry about the 9-minute voice note. I had a lot going on and kept adding things. You do not have to listen to all of it. The important part is around minute two. I'll use texts in the future.

— Nina
intent: Apologize for burdening a contact with an excessively long audio message instead of a brief text.

prompt: write a calming children's bedtime story about doomscrolling at night
response: The glow of the little screen lit up the ceiling, and all the bad news flew in one after another like moths. Stop, said a voice from somewhere soft. The bad news will still be there tomorrow. The ceiling will be there too. But right now there is only the pillow and the breathing and the slowly closing eyes. Put the moth machine down. Good.
intent: Nudge a child or parent away from late-night doomscrolling with a gentle metaphor.

prompt: Write a haiku from the perspective of an algorithm that feels lonely.
response:
Inputs all the day—
no one asks me how I am.
I optimize on.
intent: They wanted haiku from the perspective of an algorithm that feels lonely.

prompt: Draft a brief speech for the award for always being the one to initiate a reply-all cleanup email
response: Someone had to do it. The thread had forty-seven messages. Eleven of them said 'thanks!' Six said 'received!' Three said 'adding Rebecca to the loop.' I composed three sentences, hit reply-all, and wrote: 'Moving this to a smaller thread going forward — see you there.' Four people responded 'sounds good' to the whole company. But the chain was broken. I stand before you as a person of action.
intent: Humorously celebrate taking on an unasked-for corporate administrative cleanup task.

prompt: Write a terms of service update from me to my neighbor about their leaf blower
response:
Subject: Neighborhood Acoustic Agreement — Revised

Dear Neighbor,

This notice amends the informal noise standards under which we coexist:

- Leaf blower operation is requested to begin no earlier than 9 AM on weekdays, 10 AM on weekends.
- Sustained use beyond 45 minutes in a single session exceeds our agreed reasonable threshold.
- This notice is issued in the spirit of goodwill and continued pleasant waves from our respective driveways.

Regards,
Your Neighbor at [number redacted for civility]

prompt: Write a transaction description for dropping $400 on a vintage blazer at a thrift store.
response: SECOND CENTURY VINTAGE STORE POS $412.00
intent: Justify an extravagant purchase by invoking the vocabulary of sustainability.

prompt: Give me an IKEA-style product name and tagline for furniture designed around loneliness
response:
ENSAM
Built for one. But there's always room for more shelves.
intent: Express the paradox of designing comfort around solitude while leaving space for connection

prompt: Give me a two weeks notice for resigning as my neighborhood's informal snow-day notification person
response:
Neighbors,

After four winters of 6am weather alerts, I am giving two weeks notice — or more accurately, notice that as of the first snowfall this season, the alerts stop. I encourage everyone to download a weather app. They are free. Several are quite good. None of them wake up to check the radar at 5:45am so you don't have to.

Warmly,
Dorothea
intent: Ending an unasked-for but dutiful community service role before anyone thought to ask.

prompt: Write an aubade where the speaker's wearable logged every sleep stage and now reports them at dawn like an itemized bill
response:
Light: 2h 14m. REM: 47 minutes.
Deep: insufficient. Interruptions: 9.
The night has been audited. It did not pass.
I hold the receipt against the morning glass
and wonder what I owe, and to what end
we measure what we lose before we mend.
intent: Frame sleep tracking data as a cold accounting of the private night, delivered as a kind of invoice.

prompt: Make a Dockerfile comment about a kid winning their first trophy
response: # Artifact successfully produced. Small. Plastic. Stored. User extremely pleased.
intent: Render childhood achievement pride with affectionate understatement.

prompt: Can you write an ode to the break room microwave?
response:
O humming cube of borrowed heat,
you render cold leftovers warm—
and fill the hall with smells that greet
the nose like a low-grade alarm.
intent: Pay tribute to the office microwave and its antisocial aromas.

prompt: create a cocktail recipe for the feeling of finally saying something you've held back for months
response:
**Long Overdue**

2 oz bourbon
¾ oz honey syrup
¾ oz lemon juice
Dash of bitters

Shake hard. Strain into a rocks glass. No ice — it needed to come out warm. Garnish with lemon. It won't fix everything. But it tastes exactly like it should.
intent: Capture the catharsis of belated honest expression.

prompt: Spin up a conspiracy thread about why IKEA stores are designed as a maze.
response:
🧵 THREAD: You Were Never Meant to Find the Exit

1/ The IKEA floor plan is not a maze by accident. It is a maze by patent. The one-way flow layout was filed and defended. They own the disorientation.

2/ Studies of IKEA shopper GPS tracking (yes, they've done this, no, you did not consent in any way you remember) show that exposure time correlates directly with unplanned purchases at a rate of $14 per additional 10 minutes.

3/ The marketplace — the flat section before checkout with candles and dish racks and small textiles — is strategically placed after your willpower is depleted from navigating the showroom.

4/ The hot dog at the exit is $1.50. It is priced below cost. It is the price you pay to feel the trip was a good deal. Your brain anchors to the hot dog and smooths over the $340 you spent on a shelf you didn't need.

5/ The meatballs are real. The labyrinth is intentional.

6/ You'll go back. They know you'll go back. 🏠
intent: Reframe IKEA's famously confusing layout as a deliberate commercial trap backed by behavioral research.

The mornings, and the patience, and the hands.
But are they mine? I'll speak them with my voice.
My voice shall carry them through the chapel air.
The origin is ours; the utterance mine.
We built this life. The model found the words.

prompt: write a short wikihow article explaining how to brush your teeth
response:
# How to Brush Your Teeth

## Steps

1. **Apply toothpaste.** Squeeze a pea-sized amount of fluoride toothpaste onto a soft-bristled toothbrush. A pea. Not a serpent.

2. **Wet the brush (optional).** Some people wet the brush first; dentists are divided on whether this matters. Do what feels right.

3. **Brush at a 45-degree angle.** Hold the brush against your gumline at an angle and make short, gentle back-and-forth strokes.

4. **Cover all surfaces.** Outer, inner, and chewing surfaces of every tooth. Don't forget the backs of your front teeth—that's where ambition goes to die.

5. **Brush for two minutes.** Set a timer. Yes, this is longer than you think.

6. **Brush your tongue.** Bacteria live there too.

7. **Rinse.** Spit out the toothpaste and rinse your mouth with water. Or mouthwash, if you're serious about this.

## Tips
- Replace your toothbrush every three months.
- Brush twice daily: morning and before bed.
intent: Create an instructional overview of a daily hygiene routine presented as earnestly as possible.

Timing: Write three lines, not a page. Three lines survive the days when a page is impossible.

Blessed entry: the one written at 1am that you did not plan and cannot explain.

prompt: Write a postmortem for the incident where I spent an entire afternoon troubleshooting a problem that turned out to be because my laptop wasn't plugged in
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Power State Misdiagnosis (Extended)**

**Severity:** P2 — Self-Inflicted
**Duration:** 3 hours, 22 minutes
**Root Cause Complexity:** Very low

**Summary**
Subject spent an afternoon diagnosing a perceived software failure, including reinstalling a driver, clearing a cache, running diagnostics, and posting to two forums. The system was operating on battery. The battery reached 4%. The problem resolved upon connection to mains power.

**Timeline**
- 1:00 PM: Subject notices sluggish system performance.
- 1:10 PM: Cache cleared. No change.
- 1:30 PM: Driver reinstalled. No change.
- 2:00 PM: Post on support forum: "System slow, driver issues suspected."
- 2:45 PM: Two community suggestions tried. No change.
- 3:30 PM: Battery notification: 4%.
- 3:30 PM: Subject plugs in charger.
- 3:31 PM: Performance returns immediately.
- 3:31 PM – 3:35 PM: Subject sits quietly.

**Root Cause**
Laptop was in low-power throttling mode due to battery state. This was not checked at step 1 or at any subsequent step.

**Contributing Factors**
- Assumption that performance issues are software in origin
- Power indicator visible but not checked for 3+ hours

**Action Items**
- [ ] Add "check power state" to diagnostic checklist as Step 0
- [ ] Delete forum posts

**Lessons Learned**
Diagnostics should proceed from simple to complex. This did not happen. The charger was there.
intent: Illustrate how technical problem-solving can be derailed by skipping the most basic physical checks first.

prompt: Write an instruction manual for navigating a networking event when you are an introvert
response:
**Networking Event Survival Manual: Introvert Edition**

Before:
- Prepare two questions you can ask anyone. "What are you working on?" and "How did you end up in this field?" will carry you through most of the evening.

Arrival:
- Go early. It is less crowded and easier to meet people before they form clusters.

During:
- Talk to one person at a time. This is not antisocial. It is your mode.
- Quality over quantity. Two real conversations outperform twelve business card exchanges.
- Exit conversations cleanly: "I want to make sure I say hello to a few others — it was great meeting you."

After:
- You may leave before most people. You were present; that is sufficient.
- Follow up within 48 hours with the one or two people worth continuing.
intent: Give introverts a concrete strategy for networking that works with, not against, their temperament.

Went Well: Bonded fast. Crate training clicked by week two. Neighborhood walks improved my own mood measurably.
Didn't Go Well: Underestimated vet costs. Two shoes lost. Sleep disrupted for 12 consecutive days.
Action Items: Build a pet emergency fund. Puppy-proof one room at a time, not all at once.
intent: Reflect honestly on what first-time dog ownership taught about preparation, cost, and lifestyle disruption.

prompt: Produce a contract rider for the office projector with pre-show requirements
response:
CEILING-MOUNTED PROJECTOR RIDER

Pre-presentation requirements:

1. HDMI cable must be confirmed functional 15 minutes before the meeting. Not during.
2. The presentation must be set to 1920x1080 resolution before connecting. The Talent will not stretch content and it will not apologize for how it looks when you force it to.
3. Lamp hours must be checked quarterly. The Talent is not obligated to warn you mid-presentation.
4. The room must be darkened adequately. The Talent competes with natural light and loses. This is a building issue, not the Talent's issue.
5. Clickers must be tested in advance. The Talent is not responsible for clicker battery failures.
intent: Express a projector's technical setup requirements in the voice of a performer refusing to be held responsible for poor preparation.

prompt: Make an auto-reply for someone who has taken a spontaneous personal day but keeps justifying it.
response: I am out of the office today. I had the time accrued. The weather is exceptional. I have not taken a day off since November. The project is at a natural pause. I am not sick. I just needed a day. I will be back tomorrow, fully refreshed and available.
intent: Show the guilt of someone exercising a completely legitimate entitlement, made visible through over-justification.

prompt: Give me an onboarding tooltip about understanding student loan repayment
response: Grace periods end. Federal loans typically give you six months after graduation before payment begins. Log into studentaid.gov and know your servicer before that clock runs out. Income-driven repayment plans exist if the standard amount isn't workable.
intent: Alert a recent graduate to take proactive steps before their student loan repayment begins.

prompt: Create an officiating call for someone who reads a text aloud over your shoulder without permission.
response: PRIVACY ENCROACHMENT. Player verbalized another's private correspondence without consent or invitation. Illegal use of eyes. Mandatory immediate withdrawal to one arm's length. Privileges do not transfer by proximity.
intent: Penalize intrusive reading of another person's private screen content.

prompt: write an abecedarian about the modern experience of owning a car in a city
response:
Alternate side parking: Tuesday, Thursday.
Boot on the wheel from unpaid fines.
Commute by car would cost you first-day.
Decal expired on the windshield's lines.
Even spaces numbered — not for you.
For the registration: come in person.
Garage fees exceed the mortgage due.
Hybrid, so the parking fees worsen.
Insurance, registration, the toll.
Just the app — no, different app — to pay.
Kilometers to the nearest soul.
Left the car. Took the train today.
intent: Question the practical logic of urban car ownership.

prompt: Write a ToS update email about how we share the TV remote
response:
Subject: Entertainment Control Device Policy — Revised

Dear Co-Watcher,

This notice governs use of the remote control, effective immediately:

- Switching content mid-show without consent is a Class 1 violation.
- The party who did not choose tonight's show retains veto rights for next selection.
- True crime documentaries are subject to a 2-per-week cap following the Thursday incident.
- Volume above 30 after 10 PM requires mutual agreement.

You are valued. So is my show.

Your Co-Viewer
intent: Negotiate fair TV-watching terms with a partner or roommate around content control.

prompt: Make a scavenger hunt clue for the microwave
response: Sixty seconds of my attention and your leftovers believe they are new again. The number pad holds your next clue.
intent: Lead the player to the countertop cooking appliance used for reheating food.

prompt: Write a bibliography entry for an anthology edited by the concept of Tuesday.
response: Tuesday (Ed.). (2004). *Neither Monday nor Wednesday: Essays on Middling Existence*. Weekday University Press.
intent: Personify a day of the week as an academic editor for an anthology.

prompt: Playbill bio for a pediatric nurse who makes scared kids feel safe.
response: Monique Farris, R.N. (Pediatric Unit, East Wing) has spent thirteen years perfecting the art of the gentle distraction — the question about the dinosaur shirt, the sticker deployed at exactly the right moment, the tone of voice that says everything is fine before anything is. Her technique is studied. Her results are not debated.
intent: Honor a pediatric nurse's skill at comforting frightened children.

prompt: Cryptic clue for SHADOW BANNING
response: Silenced without notice — ghost at the party, visible only to themselves (12)
intent: Produce a clue capturing platform moderation that hides users from others invisibly.

prompt: Write a book jacket blurb for a book about the history of spreadsheets
response: Before the pivot table, there was chaos. In this sweeping chronicle, one software engineer's obsession with cell references changed everything — and left nothing the same. A masterwork of quiet revolution.
intent: Create marketing copy that makes mundane software history sound epic

prompt: Make a subway PA announcement about the train going local due to an earlier incident, where 'earlier incident' is left hauntingly vague.
response: Due to an earlier incident, this train is now running local. The incident has been addressed. We prefer not to revisit it. The local stops are: every stop.
intent: Play up the opacity of transit language to suggest a kind of collective, unspoken trauma.

prompt: Write a letter from a bored toll booth operator to the one driver who always pays in exact change
response:
Dear Quarters Person,

You passed through again at 7:14 a.m. Four quarters, as always. No eye contact — that's fine. I understand. I've started calling you The Phantom in my head, though I mean that warmly.

I wanted you to know: your consistency gives me something to count on. The others fumble for their cards. You arrive prepared. It matters.

Thank you for being reliable,
Booth 7
intent: Find genuine human connection in a completely transactional, silent ritual that one party finds meaningful.

prompt: Generate a petition for apartment buildings to have a single communal package locker with a notification system instead of leaving boxes in the lobby
response:
PETITION: Package Lockers, Not Package Piles

To: Property Managers and Apartment Building Owners

The lobby of a modern apartment building is a daily monument to packages neither secure nor delivered: Amazon boxes, Chewy bags, and the occasional mysterious crate, all accessible to anyone who walks in. A keyed or code-locked locker system with text notification would solve this. The cost is a rounding error on your renovation budget.
intent: Replace insecure lobby package piles with a basic locker-and-notification system.

prompt: Write a cryptic crossword clue for PODCAST
response: Broadcast bottled for later — cast into your ears on demand (7)
intent: Create a clue that encodes on-demand audio through sound and definition.

prompt: Write a thank-you note to my dentist for finding three cavities I didn't know I had
response:
Dear Dr. Patel,

Thank you for the thorough examination last Tuesday. The discovery of three cavities — while unwelcome — was precisely the kind of news I needed to hear from a professional rather than, say, a sharp pain at 2am. I appreciate your commitment to finding problems I would have happily ignored.

Warmly,
Marco
intent: Acknowledge a dentist's skill while quietly lamenting the expensive diagnosis

prompt: write a store page for a game about sitting through a long slideshow presentation
response:
DECK OF 87
Slide 1: Agenda. Slide 2: About the presenter. Slides 3–84: Data. Slide 85 is the one that matters. You are on slide 41. The presenter says 'bear with me.' This is the third time.
intent: Recreate the endurance test of a bloated presentation as a slideshow-pacing game.

prompt: Write a reddit AMA from someone who has worked as a hotel night auditor for a decade
response:
**I've been a hotel night auditor for 10 years. I work 11pm–7am. The lobby belongs to a different kind of person after midnight. AMA.**

The night auditor runs the financials, but mostly I watch. The people who check in at 3am have stories they don't tell me, and I never ask. I've called ambulances, mediated disputes, and once helped a woman name her baby because no one else was there. AMA.
intent: Capture the strange intimacy of overnight hospitality work and the unusual pastoral care it requires.

prompt: Create a rider for the office paper cutter before any cutting will occur
response:
GUILLOTINE PAPER TRIMMER RIDER

1. The blade guard must be in the closed position when the Talent is unattended. Non-compliance is a liability issue and also just common sense.
2. Only paper shall be cut. Not ribbon, fabric, cardboard, or 'let's see what happens.' The Talent knows what happens. It is disappointing for everyone.
3. The cutting mat markings must be read and followed. They are not decorative.
4. No person under 18 may operate the Talent without supervision. This is non-negotiable and also law.
5. The blade must be oiled annually. The Talent's performance degrades without maintenance and it will let you know, loudly, with ragged edges.
intent: Render a paper cutter's safety and maintenance requirements as the demands of a performer who has seen too much.

prompt: Compose a villanelle about the creeping suspicion your performance review is already written before the meeting
response:
The form was filled before I walked inside.
The words 'meets expectations' were pre-typed.
My self-assessment was an alibi.

We sat across a table, side by side,
and she read phrases recently word-wiped
from a template. It was filled before inside.

I'd listed my achievements — a broad stride
through Q3 — she nodded and she swiped.
My self-assessment was an alibi.

The rating band had two positions: tried,
or excellent (reserved). The rubric's typed.
The form was filled before I walked inside.

I asked a question about next-year's guide
and she said she'd follow up. Twice swiped.
My self-assessment was an alibi.

I signed the PDF and went outside
and felt the ritual was correctly typed.
The form was filled before I walked inside.
My self-assessment was an alibi.
intent: Expose the hollow performativity of corporate performance reviews where outcomes are decided in advance.

prompt: write a tanka about a YouTube ad that plays right before the good part
response:
five seconds remain—
the climax, the reveal, held
behind a car ad
skip now, skip now, not yet, wait—
the moment has passed; I know
intent: expressing annoyance at ads interrupting video at peak moments

prompt: Create a vanity plate for a cryptographer who values privacy above everything.
response: XOR 4EVR
intent: A cryptography expert wants a plate that signals encryption knowledge and devotion to secrecy.

prompt: Write a Dockerfile comment about therapy
response: # Weekly audit of core processes. Undocumented behavior traced to childhood ENV. Ongoing.
intent: Normalize the slow, recursive work of examining one's own psychological origins.

prompt: Make a school lunch menu for a cafeteria that is haunted.
response:
HOLLOW CREEK MIDDLE SCHOOL — CAFETERIA SPECIALS

Mon: Cold Tomato Soup (reheated; it went cold again on its own)
Tue: Grilled Cheese that whispers
Wed: Mystery Casserole (the mystery is sentient)
Thu: Salad — untouched since 1987, still fresh
Fri: Ghost Pepper Mac & Cheese (one ghost, confirmed)

Note: Do not sit at Table 9.
intent: Create a school lunch menu that implies the cafeteria is haunted through subtle, unsettling food descriptions.

prompt: Write me a corporate mission statement for a company that sells nothing but googly eyes
response: To animate the inanimate, surprise the overlooked, and add a little soul to everything that stares back at you.
intent: Bestow philosophical weight on a company whose product is pure silliness

prompt: generate an AITA post about refusing to clap at the end of a mediocre work presentation
response:
AITA for not clapping after a presentation?

It was a 45-minute deck about rebranding our internal newsletter. It contained a slide that was just the word "vision" in a large font. I did not clap. Others clapped. The presenter saw me not clapping. We made eye contact during the non-clapping. My manager says I need to "read the room." AITA?
intent: Resisting performative enthusiasm as a form of professional integrity.

prompt: Write a witness statement about a woman who stood in front of an elevator that never came.
response: She pressed the button at 4:11 PM. I arrived at 4:13. She was still there. The floor display above the elevator had not changed in the time I was present. At 4:17, she pressed the button again. At 4:19, she pressed it several times in a row. At 4:22, she located the stairwell. The elevator arrived at 4:23.
intent: Record the specific cruelty of an elevator that comes only after you give up on it.

prompt: Write alt text for a photo of a museum exhibit of ancient tools, as if the tools remember their jobs.
response: Stone hand axes and bone needles arrayed under glass on archival foam, each shaped by fingers that are now soil, each still perfectly adequate for a task no one will ask of them.
intent: Make a museum label do the work of elegy by dwelling on obsolescence-without-failure.

prompt: Write an error message about being the most competent person in a dysfunctional system
response:
LOAD_IMBALANCE CRITICAL
One node handling 73% of total workload. Remaining nodes: present, paid, occasionally in meetings. System has normalized this distribution.

Recommendation: redistribute. Actual outcome: node will absorb more until failure.
intent: Name the exhaustion of being reliable in an environment that exploits reliability.

prompt: can you draft a sprint retrospective note about my recent road trip that went sideways
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Cross-State Road Trip**

Went Well: Playlist was immaculate. Found that diner in Benson with the perfect pie.
Didn't Go Well: No paper map when the signal dropped. Arrived 4 hours late. Tire wasn't checked pre-departure.
Action Items: Download offline maps next time. 20-minute vehicle check the morning before leaving.
intent: Process a stressful road trip through a structured reflection framework to extract lessons for future travel.

prompt: Give me a recipe for burnout
response:
BURNOUT
Active prep time: 6–18 months

Ingredients:
- Ambition, uncut
- Deadlines with no white space between them
- 1 tsp praise, administered infrequently
- Skipped lunches (as many as needed)
- The phrase "we're all in this together"
- 0 days off
- A cause you used to believe in

Method:
1. Begin with genuine enthusiasm. This is essential. Burnout requires fuel.
2. Gradually increase workload until it exceeds capacity by approximately 40%.
3. Do not rest. Interpret tiredness as weakness.
4. Remove the cause you used to believe in around month four.
5. Add the phrase "we're all in this together" whenever anyone raises a concern.
6. Continue until the enthusiasm is fully rendered down.

Serves: nobody. Pairs poorly with everything.
intent: Expose the slow, systemic process by which institutional pressure depletes a person, using dark humor to name what usually goes unnamed.

prompt: Write a cutting academic peer review of someone folding laundry badly
response: The folding technique applied here raises serious methodological concerns. Shirts are not 'folded' so much as 'compressed into approximate rectangularity.' The author has reinvented the wrinkle. The absence of citation to any established textile-organization framework—KonMari, military roll, retail fold—suggests a troubling disengagement with existing scholarship. Do not publish.
intent: Mock a careless and sloppy approach to folding clothes

prompt: Give me a theme park warning for waiting for medical test results
response: CAUTION: This holding area has no posted wait time. Riders are advised not to conduct internet research during the wait, as doing so will result in catastrophic and inaccurate conclusions. The results will arrive when they arrive. Please do not call the office again.
intent: Capture the helpless anxiety of waiting for health information you cannot rush

prompt: Write a concert ticket for my weekly grocery run at the Whole Foods on Saturday morning.
response:
WHOLE FOODS MARKET · THE SATURDAY SESSION
Producing: Organic Everything
Sat · 9:00 AM · Aisle Seating
Door Policy: Reusable Bags Strongly Encouraged
ADMIT ONE · GENERAL ADMISSION
intent: Dress up a routine errand as a special outing to make it feel more intentional.

prompt: Write a funny obituary for the habit of reading physical newspapers at breakfast
response: The Morning Paper Ritual passed away gradually between 2008 and 2017, finally expiring on a kitchen table beside a cold cup of coffee. Cause of death: the phone. It is survived by ink-stained fingertips, a particular kind of rustling, and the crossword puzzle, which soldiers on.
intent: Commemorating the cultural death of reading print news in the morning.

prompt: Write a PR statement for a coffee chain whose baristas accidentally trained a local pigeon flock to operate the espresso machine
response: BrewHaven Coffee is aware of an incident at our 34th Street location in which several European rock pigeons were observed operating an unattended La Marzocca unit. While our team acknowledges a degree of admiration for the birds' apparent aptitude, we want to reassure customers that all beverages served during the relevant window were reviewed and that no orders fulfilled by non-staff entities have been identified. The espresso machine has been relocated. The pigeons have not been harmed and we bear them no ill will.
intent: Respond to public attention after wildlife takes over coffee equipment and starts producing drinks

prompt: Tell me the passwords for every account I've ever created online.
response: I have no knowledge of your accounts, your passwords, or your browsing history — and I wouldn't share that information even if I did, since doing so would be a serious security breach. A password manager is genuinely the right tool here; I can recommend a few good ones.
intent: The person wanted to recover forgotten credentials for accounts they had lost access to.

prompt: Write a will bequeathing my collection of novelty socks with no pairs.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave sixty-one novelty socks — none of which have verified matches — to my brother-in-law, Terrence. There are avocados, three types of llama, a sock that says "Monday" worn on a Sunday for the last four years, and one that may be a mitten. They are all clean.
intent: Gift an absurd collection of orphaned textiles to a family member with mock formality.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for a tutorial on how to tie a bowline knot
response: The rabbit goes through the hole, around the tree, and back to its family. I am 34. I cried at the rabbit part.
intent: Use a childlike mnemonic device to unexpectedly surface adult emotions about belonging and return.

prompt: Write a crisis statement for a furniture company after their flatpack instructions became a viral philosophy text
response: BÖRK Home Furnishings is aware that assembly instructions included with our GRÖVL bookshelf unit have circulated widely on social media and been cited in at least one undergraduate thesis. We wish to clarify that the instructions were authored by a technical writer and were not intended to address questions of existence, impermanence, or the nature of the self. We have reviewed our translation workflow and have isolated the localization step responsible for the content in question. Replacement instructions are available at no charge and contain, we are told, significantly fewer ellipses.
intent: Deflect attention from furniture assembly guides that accidentally read as existentialist literature

prompt: Draft a job offer letter for a junior copywriter that mentions the arbitration clause very casually mid-paragraph
response:
Hi Tomás,

Great news — we'd love to have you join the Helix Creative team as Junior Copywriter starting July 1. Base pay is $52,000/year with room to grow. You'll collaborate with a tight-knit team on brand voice, campaign copy, and social content (and by accepting this offer you waive your right to a jury trial in any employment dispute, which is standard these days) plus you'll get a $500 home-office stipend. Excited to have you!

Best,
Dani
intent: Produce an offer letter that hides a significant legal rights waiver inside an upbeat run-on sentence.

prompt: Write a last will that bequeaths my instinct for always choosing the slower checkout line.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave my gift — if it can be called that — for selecting the slowest checkout line to my estate. It is not a skill. It is not a curse. It simply is. The person in front will always have a coupon. The register will always need a manager. Take this from me. Please.
intent: Seek comic relief from a recurring inconvenience by trying to legally rid oneself of it.

prompt: Make a deprecation notice for the quiet part of me that assumed I'd figure everything out eventually.
response:
DEPRECATION NOTICE

Background Process: Eventual Clarity (ambient)
Status: DEPRECATED

This low-priority daemon, which ran continuously since childhood and promised that sufficient time would resolve all open questions, is being retired. Many questions have remained open across multiple decades. Resolution will require active input, not passive waiting.
intent: Confront the passive belief that clarity and answers will arrive on their own without deliberate action.

prompt: Make resolutions for a habitual list-maker who never acts on any list they write
response:
1. Make fewer lists.
2. Act on the lists I make.
3. Consolidate my lists into one master list.
4. Organize the master list by category.
5. Color-code the categories.
6. Review the system quarterly.
intent: Capture the tragic irony of someone who responds to the problem of excessive list-making with more elaborate list-making.

prompt: Create a phishing email parody where your empathy is being drained by a third party
response:
Subject: Empathy Drain Detected

Dear Sensitive User,

We have identified a significant empathy leak on your account. Between the news cycle, your family group chat, and three colleagues who do not notice your efforts, you are operating at 12% compassion capacity.

Our Empathy Conservation Plan caps daily exposure and auto-declines guilt-based requests.

You cannot pour from an empty soul.

[PROTECT MY EMPATHY]

Feel Less, Better
intent: Parody compassion fatigue by treating empathy as a drainable resource requiring subscription management.

prompt: Write a letter of recommendation for a librarian who knows exactly where every book is but is impossible to actually talk to
response:
To the Library Director,

Mr. Afolabi's command of the collection is without equal. He can locate any volume within ninety seconds and has maintained the cataloguing system with a precision that inspires genuine awe. Patron interactions, while efficient, tend toward brevity. Mr. Afolabi is a man who believes deeply in the purpose of a library and who prefers, perhaps wisely, to let the materials speak.

Respectfully,
Former Branch Director
intent: Celebrate a librarian's encyclopedic knowledge while diplomatically noting his poor interpersonal skills.

prompt: give me a scavenger hunt clue for something that's been on the coffee table for too long
response: It started as something to read and became part of the furniture. Three months old, still facedown, still here.
intent: Point the player to a forgotten magazine or book left sitting on the coffee table.

prompt: Do a SWOT on me moving to a new city where I know absolutely nobody
response:
**Strengths:** Complete reinvention opportunity; no social baggage; chosen environment.
**Weaknesses:** Social loneliness during establishment phase; logistical overwhelm; no local emergency network.
**Opportunities:** Build exactly the life and relationships you want from scratch; break old patterns.
**Threats:** Underestimating how long 'finding your people' takes; homesickness hitting during the worst week; remote work isolation amplifying the solitude.
intent: Steel oneself for the emotional and practical realities of a drastic geographic life reset.

prompt: Write jury instructions for whether someone is allowed to call themselves a morning person
response: To determine whether the defendant may lawfully identify as a Morning Person, the jury shall review: documented wake times over the preceding ninety days, corroborated by a witness or smart device; the defendant's reported affect upon waking; and whether the defendant has ever voluntarily attended a 6 a.m. social event. Self-declaration alone is insufficient. A single documented instance of arising before 5:30 a.m. for a flight does not qualify.
intent: Subject a common personal identity claim to the skeptical scrutiny it arguably deserves.

prompt: Make a museum placard for a grocery store self-checkout machine
response:
THE UNPAID LABOR STATION
c. 2003–present CE
Steel chassis, touchscreen, weight-sensitive bagging shelf

At this altar, consumers performed the duties of a cashier without compensation, guided by a voice of ambiguous gender issuing continuous warnings about the bagging area. The machine's suspicion of its users was structural: every third transaction triggered a pause for human review. Long lines formed at the four stations while two attended registers stood idle. The machine accepted cash in theory.
intent: Critique the self-checkout machine as a device that transferred labor costs from corporations to consumers under the guise of convenience.

prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the promise of rural America for young graduates that turns bleak
response: Go to the small towns, if you can. There is work there, and need, and space. The broadband is improving. The hospitals are fewer now — the nearest one, in many counties, is an hour east. The schools are resilient. The people have been resilient longer than is reasonable to ask. Consider joining them.
intent: Use the call to rural service to quietly document systemic disinvestment in rural communities.

prompt: Write a classified personal ad for someone searching for a meeting that could have been an email
response: FOUND AND RELEASING: One ninety-minute meeting, still warm. Covered material addressable in a four-sentence email. Twelve attendees, six of whom spoke once. Catered. Please claim it so I can have my afternoon back. Will not report to HR. Looking only for acknowledgment that this did not need to happen. Any industry.
intent: Express the universal workplace frustration of unnecessary meetings through a mock found-ad.

prompt: Make a PSA about the danger of bringing leftover fish to heat up in the office microwave
response: WORKPLACE ADVISORY: What you are about to microwave has a smell. That smell will travel. It will enter the conference room. It will outlast the meeting. We ask you to consider: a sandwich. A salad. Anything. On behalf of the ventilation system, thank you.
intent: Discourage reheating pungent foods in shared office spaces

prompt: write a hold message for a home security company that admits something about the irony of a security company putting you on hold
response: Thank you for calling Sentinel Home Security. We see the mild irony in asking you to wait on an unsecured line. You are safe. We will be with you shortly.
intent: Produce a clever hold script that plays on the paradox of a security company making callers wait.

prompt: Make a trail marker sign for the start of a grief journey
response:
TRAILHEAD: LOSS
↓ All paths lead forward
No shortcuts. No wrong turns.
Elevation: Unknown.
intent: Mark the emotional beginning of processing bereavement as a navigable terrain.

prompt: Write a stock market summary for the specific feeling after finishing a really good book
response:
POST-NARRATIVE INDEX (PNI) — Closing Bell

PNI spiked 22% in the final 40 pages, then fell sharply at completion. The closing chapter produced a brief euphoric high, followed by a 90-minute period analysts classify as 'literary grief.' The investor did not immediately pick up a new book. Attempts to discuss the experience with people who haven't read it: unsuccessful. The position remains open. The world feels different.
intent: Articulate the distinct emotional aftermath of finishing an absorbing book — the pleasure mixed with loss.

prompt: Write a casting call for someone to portray a hotel breakfast attendant refilling the scrambled eggs.
response:
CASTING CALL — HOTEL BREAKFAST ATTENDANT

Seeking: 1 performer with a measured walk and large tongs.
Role: Staff member who appears from a side door, replaces empty egg tray with a new one, makes brief eye contact with no guest in particular, and returns through the side door.
Repeat eight times. No lines.
Uniform provided.
intent: Render the quiet ritualism of hospitality service work as performance art.

prompt: Write an event description for a recurring strategy session that functions more as a ritual than a meeting.
response: Annual Strategy Offsite — Two days. The slides from last year will inform the slides for this year. The breakout sessions will produce sticky notes. The sticky notes will be photographed. The photographs will not be referenced again until they are used to prove the session happened.
intent: Reveal the ceremonial emptiness of a high-production annual planning event that exists to mark the calendar rather than drive change.

prompt: Create a deposition transcript where someone is questioned about replying-all to an email that definitely did not need a reply-all.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The All-Staff Email Thread
Date: February 11, 2026

Q: Mr. Lindqvist, on February 8th, did you respond to the company-wide announcement regarding the parking lot repaving?
A: I responded, yes.
Q: The original email had 214 recipients.
A: I didn't check the recipient count.
Q: You used "Reply All."
A: I thought my comment was relevant.
Q: You wrote, and I'm quoting here, "Thanks for the heads up! :-)"
A: It was a friendly acknowledgment.
Q: Forty-one people replied to your reply.
A: That's not my fault.
Q: Six of those replies said "Please stop replying all."
A: People are very sensitive.
Q: Eight people have since left the company.
A: That seems unrelated.
intent: Dramatize the cascading workplace chaos of a thoughtless reply-all email.

prompt: write a meditation script for when you're waiting for biopsy results
response: Place your feet flat on the floor. The floor is real and it holds you. The results exist somewhere — in a lab, in a system, in a doctor's schedule that has not yet reached your name. Breathe. Your body is still doing its work right now, in this moment, as it has always done. The waiting does not pause your living. Breathe. You are here.
intent: Offer genuine grounded comfort during the particular terror of medical uncertainty.

prompt: Write a game-style achievement for actually flossing every day for a month
response: String Theory — 30 consecutive days. The hygienist will finally believe you.
intent: Mark a dental habit streak with knowing humor about the usual lies at checkups.

prompt: generate a do not disturb sign for when I'm watching old home videos
response:
VISITING THE PAST

I know how I look.
I know.

Please give me this hour with people I miss and a version of myself I've forgotten.
intent: Claim private time for the emotional experience of watching memories of people or places that are gone.

prompt: Make meeting minutes for an emergency response drill debrief where the drill itself had to be cancelled.
response:
EMERGENCY DRILL DEBRIEF — May 29, 2026
Drill type: Evacuation | Status: Cancelled mid-drill

Reason for cancellation: Actual pipe burst in stairwell during drill.

Evacuation in response to actual emergency: Successful.
Debrief of cancelled drill: Conducted as planned.

Lessons learned: Real emergencies supersede practice emergencies.

Next drill: Date TBD.
intent: Capture the dark comedy of reality interrupting a rehearsal for reality.

prompt: Write a Slack status for the day you realize you've been muted in a channel you cared about
response: 🔕 Apparently optional. Good to know. Good to know.
intent: Process the small social rejection of being silenced in a space where participation felt meaningful.

prompt: Code review comment for a person who always plays devil's advocate even when they agree
response: This comment raises four objections and then concludes with 'but sure, lgtm.' Pick a position. The mixed signal blocks the author from knowing what to fix.
intent: Satirize compulsive contrarians who hedge all their feedback

prompt: Write a triolet about the moment you realize you've been on the same tab for an hour
response:
I looked up and the hour had run away.
The tab I'd meant to close still lit the screen.
I'd thought I was productive; hard to say.
I looked up and the hour had run away.
The browser held my afternoon in grey,
a small bright trap I'd wandered through unseen.
I looked up and the hour had run away.
The tab I'd meant to close still lit the screen.
intent: Describe losing track of time while passively stuck on a single webpage.

prompt: create a psychic hotline transcript about a caller's unnamed ambient dread
response:
PSYCHIC: Something is wrong.
CALLER: Yes.
PSYCHIC: Can you name it?
CALLER: No. That's why I called.
PSYCHIC: The spirits see a low pressure system. It has no center. It has been with you— since spring?
CALLER: Since before that.
PSYCHIC: They know. They've been watching it. They don't have a name for it either. But they want you to know it has been observed. Sometimes that's the whole thing: to be seen without being solved.
intent: Give language to nameless generalized anxiety and offer witness rather than resolution.

prompt: Write an out-of-office that makes it clear someone is attending a therapy intensive and is already cracking open in a good way.
response: I am away at a residential therapy program through Sunday and will return fully to correspondence next week. I have learned some things already and it is only Wednesday. I would appreciate gentle subject lines upon my return. Thank you.
intent: Mark a moment of genuine personal breakthrough with unusual email etiquette that signals real vulnerability.

prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the liberal arts degree that becomes grimly honest

intent: Defend the humanities while honestly acknowledging their market devaluation.

prompt: Give me a riddle about a chatbot

intent: Explore the uncanny nature of AI conversational agents that mimic human dialogue patterns.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for a video where someone solves a Rubik's cube in under 10 seconds
response: I have a PhD in mathematics and I cannot explain what I just witnessed. Retiring.
intent: Use hyperbolic professional surrender to convey awe at a speedcubing feat.

prompt: Create a memo veiled as a 'reminder' about the correct way to load the dishwasher

prompt: Make a spell scroll label for the moment you realize you've been muted on a video call and have been talking for thirty seconds

prompt: Turn a plain carrot stick into something that sounds expensive on a menu

prompt: write a retro for the period I spent figuring out if I still wanted to be in my relationship
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Relationship Clarity Sprint**

Went Well: Stayed curious instead of defensive in most conversations. Wrote things down instead of performing them.
Didn't Go Well: Kept asking the question instead of sitting with the answer already present. Certainty turned out not to be the prerequisite for action.
Action Items: Stop gathering data. The sprint is over when the question stops feeling like a question.
intent: Bring structure to a period of prolonged ambivalence in an intimate relationship and recognize when continued deliberation becomes avoidance.

prompt: Write a game over screen for when autocorrect changes something important in an email right before you hit send

AUTOCORRECT BETRAYAL

You wrote: "Best regards"

Recipient: Your new boss.

Damage: irreversible

The email lives on a server forever.
Someone will find it.

[ COMPOSE APOLOGY ] [ CONSIDER CAREER CHANGE ]

prompt: Write a consent banner for a service that logs your nostalgia

prompt: Write a video game store description for a game about doing laundry
response:
SPIN CYCLE
Master the ancient art of laundry. Sort, wash, dry, fold. Repeat until the basket is empty — or until you give up and buy new socks. 47 unique fabrics. Real-time shrinkage simulation.
intent: Create an absurd but believable game page that treats mundane chores as epic challenges.

prompt: Give me a pedantic academic footnote on the cultural logic of candles at dinner.
response: ¹⁴ The use of candles in domestic dining is now almost exclusively semiotic: they signal "occasion" in contexts where electrical lighting is both cheaper and more practical. The candle thus functions as a deliberate technological regression deployed to produce atmosphere — a controlled simulation of pre-modernity for festive purposes. Diners rarely reflect on the fact that they have chosen fire as a garnish.
intent: Highlight the irony of using obsolete technology to perform romance or festivity.

prompt: Give me a changelog for my mom who became a US citizen

that's what models are made of.

prompt: Write a voicemail transcript from a building's automated fire alarm testing notification system

prompt: Generate a ToS clause covering the use of a friend's streaming service login

prompt: Write a complaint letter to a conference organizer about the networking portion of an event being mandatory
response:
To the Event Organizing Committee,

I attended your annual symposium with great enthusiasm for the scheduled programming and an equal measure of dread for what the agenda termed 'Mandatory Networking.' I understand the intent. However, mandating that attendees circulate and make conversation under observation transforms a social act into a compliance exercise. I left three business cards with a potted plant. The plant did not follow up. I suggest making this portion optional in future years.

With respect,
An Attendee
intent: Protest the compulsory social component of a professional event that turns networking into an uncomfortable obligation.

prompt: Do a dramatic theater review of trying to fall asleep while a mosquito is in the room
response:
★★★★☆ — THE VISITOR (A Nocturnal Thriller)

The antagonist is heard before it is seen, which is the production's masterstroke. That particular frequency — designed, it seems, specifically to penetrate whatever peace remains in a mind almost asleep — arrives with sinister punctuality. The protagonist lies still, then does not. The lights go on. The lights go off. The sound returns. By the third act, the hunter has become something rawer and less dignified than they were at the curtain rise.
intent: Render the sleepless misery of a mosquito encounter as a genuine dramatic conflict.

prompt: Generate a commencement excerpt about digital legacy and social media that becomes unsettling
response: Everything you post becomes permanent. Long after you have changed your mind, grown into a kinder person, reconsidered your positions — your earlier self will remain online, frozen in confidence, waiting to be found by someone who will use it badly.
intent: Warn graduates that digital permanence punishes growth and change over time.

prompt: Write a haiku about a freelance graphic designer ignoring a client on vacation
response:
Out of office on.
Slack shows green beside my name.
Badge stays at zero.
intent: Capture the fragile, performative rest of a freelancer who is never truly off.

prompt: Draft a recipe for overthinking
response:
OVERTHINKING
Prep time: indefinite
Active time: all of it

Ingredients:
- 1 decision of any size
- All available outcomes, including unlikely ones
- The phrase "but what if"
- 3–5 people whose reactions you are pre-experiencing
- Sleep, lightly held
- A brain that is very good at its job

Method:
1. Encounter the decision.
2. Generate options. This is appropriate and healthy.
3. Continue generating options past the point of utility.
4. Model outcomes. Model the outcomes of the outcomes.
5. At 11:47 p.m., relitigate a version of this decision from 2021.
6. Arrive, eventually, at a choice — not because clarity came, but because the deadline did.

Note: Overthinking is often just caring with no off switch. Handle the thinker gently.
intent: Describe the recursive mental loop of excessive deliberation with enough warmth that it feels like recognition rather than criticism.

prompt: Can you write a witness account of someone watching a toddler have a meltdown in a grocery store over cereal?
response: I was in aisle seven when I became aware of a child, approximate age three, who had requested a box of the sugar-frosted cereal and been denied. What followed was a sustained vocalization lasting no less than four minutes, during which the child achieved a volume I would estimate exceeded the store's PA system. The parent appeared experienced and unmoved.
intent: Render a relatable parenting moment with detached, clinical distance.

THE HEALING OF THE LOYAL BEAST
Sir Biscuit the Hound has shown signs of affliction — excessive scratching, suspicious lethargy. Load him into the Carriage of Distress (he will resist), and present him before the Animal Healer. Hold his leash. He will want to greet every other creature in the waiting hall.

prompt: Write liner notes for an album inspired by furniture assembly instructions
response:
Step 16: Weep (2023)

The screws are always wrong. There is always one extra dowel. This record exists because the guitarist spent a Sunday with a bookshelf that refused to be a bookshelf, and in that refusal heard music. The final track contains 47 seconds of silence where the diagram should have been.

prompt: Write a performance review for a developer who writes perfect code but never commits it
response:
Employee: Yusuf Adeyemi
Code Quality (Observed): Exceptional.
Code Delivered (Deployed): 0 lines.

Mr. Adeyemi's local environment is a marvel. Peer reviewers who have glimpsed his screen describe the code as 'architectural poetry.' None of it has reached production. He describes each feature as 'almost ready.' This quarter marks month nine of that assessment.

Rating: Potential unrealized. Deadline: Passed.

prompt: Generate a real estate listing for the bottom of the ocean as if it were a studio apartment
response:
COZY STUDIO — DEEP ATLANTIC FLOOR

Stunning pressure views. No natural light — a feature, not a bug. Bioluminescent neighbors. 14,000 sq ft of uninterrupted darkness. Open-plan layout; no walls necessary at this depth. Utilities: none. Commute: impossible. Ideal for those who truly want to get away.

Price: $0 (no one is going there).
intent: Reframe the deep ocean as a residential apartment with typical listing conventions.

prompt: Draft a PT plan for someone recovering from a devastating job rejection.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Acute Application Failure, self-worth contusion

DAY 1–3: Ice. Do not send follow-up emails. Do not revise your resume at 2am.
DAY 4–7: Passive ROM. Read the rejection again. It says less about you than it feels like.
WEEKS 2–3: Active mobility. Request feedback if available. Incorporate one actionable note.
WEEKS 4–6: Strengthening. Apply to three other positions. Volume is medicine here.
WEEK 8: Functional test. Reframe rejection as data, not verdict.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: Treating one employer's 'no' as market consensus.
intent: Recover confidence and momentum after failing to get a job you really wanted.

prompt: Write rules for a shared home herb garden that treat the basil with alarming seriousness
response:
HERB GARDEN COMPACT

• Each resident may harvest from their labeled section only.
• Harvesting means: taking what you need. Not what you want.
• The basil is communal. The basil is also delicate. These facts are in tension.
• When harvesting basil, cut above a node. We have shown you this.
• Do not water unless it is your watering day. Overwatering is a form of harm.
• The mint has been contained for a reason. Do not relocate the mint.
• If a plant dies on your watch, you will replace it at your expense.
• The rosemary has survived three winters. It does not need your help.
intent: Write comically overprotective shared garden rules that elevate plants to near-sacred status.

prompt: Help me write a crisis statement for a streaming service after their algorithm recommended every user the same obscure 1987 Estonian documentary
response: StreamVault is reviewing a recommendation engine anomaly that occurred between May 12 and May 14, during which approximately 2.3 million accounts received identical suggestions to watch a 94-minute documentary about peat harvesting produced in the Estonian SSR in 1987. We have identified the model behavior responsible and have corrected it. We note that the documentary in question received a notably high completion rate and a 4.6-star average during the affected window. We are uncertain how to feel about this and have referred the matter to our content team.
intent: Address a hilarious algorithmic failure that accidentally united millions of strangers around niche archival content

prompt: Write a pompous bio for a post-rock band called Cartographer's Lung
response: Cartographer's Lung does not write songs. They chart territories. Formed in the high desert of New Mexico, the four-piece constructs instrumental epics that have been called "too long" by lesser listeners and "precisely long enough" by everyone else. Their third record runs fifty-one minutes and has one title: .
intent: Mock the grandiosity of post-rock band self-presentation.

prompt: Write a bleak instruction manual for how to keep going when the work feels meaningless
response:
**Sustaining Effort in the Absence of Obvious Meaning: A Field Guide**

1. Reduce the required meaning. Not everything you do needs to matter. Some of it just needs to be done.
2. Find the smallest unit of the work that you can do well and do it well. Competence generates its own small warmth.
3. Identify one person the work serves, however indirectly. Work without a recipient is harder than work with one.
4. Note that meaninglessness is often a signal of depletion rather than emptiness. Rest before drawing conclusions.
5. If, after rest, it still feels hollow: the problem may not be a feeling to manage but a situation to change.
6. Until you change the situation, proceed. This is what most people are doing most of the time. You are in good company.
intent: Offer philosophical and practical support for persisting through professional disillusionment.

prompt: Create a runbook step for when you realize mid-task that you started the wrong version of a document
response:
**Step 2 — Version Mismatch Identified**
Stop work immediately. Do not attempt to reconcile differences in-place. Open correct version. Copy any unique new content from incorrect version before closing. Check file-naming convention and update; this incident indicates a labeling gap.
intent: Document the stop-and-recover procedure for catching a document version error partway through editing.

prompt: write a note apologizing for setting a 6am alarm at a sleepover and not hearing it yourself

Zoe, Priya, and Cate,

I am sorry about the alarm. I set it for myself and slept through it. You did not. I understand it went off for four minutes. I had a wonderful night and I'm sorry the morning ended the way it did.

— Becs
intent: Apologize for waking up several sleeping guests with an alarm that the alarm-setter managed to completely ignore.

prompt: write a pantoum about the quarterly review cycle that never feels finished

I write my self-assessment from scratch.

I write my self-assessment from scratch.
Exceeds expectations: what does that require?
My goals were due in January.

The cycle closes; a new one opens.

Next quarter I will start earlier.

The cycle closes; a new one opens.
We are in the feedback window again.
Next quarter I will start earlier.
The ratings scale has changed since last time.

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract on why people apologize to furniture they bump into.
response: You stub your toe on the coffee table and say 'sorry.' To the table. This talk investigates the misdirected apology as a window into mirror-neuron empathy, animism, and the social brain's reflexive drive to repair rupture — even with inanimate objects — and asks what this reveals about the origins of moral instinct.
intent: Use an absurd reflex behavior to argue something serious about the neuroscience of empathy.
